The half-pipe here is competition grade, and the progression of parks means riders of all levels can safely push their limits. New Zealand's highest cafe sits at the summit, and on a clear day you can see forever.

reviews.summary: The Cardrona Alpine Resort adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Family-favourite ski resort with top terrain parks between Queenstown and Wanaka. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Skydive Wanaka
pois.short: Skydive over Lake Wanaka with Mount Aspiring and the Southern Alps as your backdrop.
pois.commentary: Skydive Wanaka offers tandem jumps from up to 15,000 feet with views of Lake Wanaka, Mount Aspiring, and the full sweep of the Southern Alps. Freefall lasts up to 60 seconds with a scenic parachute descent of 5-7 minutes over the lake and mountains.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Wanaka Airport, and the sky above you is about to become your playground. Skydive Wanaka will take you up to 15,000 feet — that's nearly three miles above the earth — where you'll step out of the plane into thin, cold air with one of the most spectacular views in all of New Zealand spread below. Lake Wanaka, Lake Hawea, Mount Aspiring, the Southern Alps — it's all there, a living map of mountains and water. You'll freefall for up to 60 seconds, the wind roaring at 200 kilometres per hour, before the chute opens and everything goes quiet. The five-minute parachute ride down is pure bliss, floating above the lake with snow-capped peaks in every direction. Many say this is the most scenic skydive in the world.

reviews.summary: The Skydive Wanaka adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Skydive over Lake Wanaka with Mount Aspiring and the Southern Alps as your backdrop. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Wanaka Rock Climbing & Via Ferrata
pois.short: Climb natural rock faces and a thrilling via ferrata with Lake Wanaka views.
pois.commentary: The Wanaka region offers world-class rock climbing at crags like Hospital Flat and Roadside, plus guided via ferrata experiences on Wildwire's twin waterfall route. The via ferrata ascends alongside Twin Falls, combining climbing rungs, wire bridges, and waterfall exposure at heights up to 350 metres.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in Wanaka's climbing heartland, where schist rock faces and a world-class via ferrata await. If you've never tried climbing, the guided crags at Hospital Flat offer perfect introductory routes on grippy schist with mountain views. But the real showstopper is the Wildwire via ferrata — a series of iron rungs, ladders, and wire bridges bolted into the cliff face beside a twin waterfall. You'll climb up to 350 metres, crossing swing bridges above cascading water with Lake Wanaka glittering far below. It's a physical challenge, but the via ferrata system means you're always clipped to a safety cable. The sensation of climbing alongside a roaring waterfall, spray on your face, mountains all around — it's genuinely unforgettable.

reviews.summary: The Wanaka Rock Climbing & Via Ferrata adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Climb natural rock faces and a thrilling via ferrata with Lake Wanaka views. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Stand-Up Paddleboarding on Lake Wanaka
pois.short: Glide across Lake Wanaka's pristine waters on a stand-up paddleboard.
pois.commentary: Stand-up paddleboarding on Lake Wanaka offers a serene way to explore the lake's bays, islands, and famous lone tree. Rental and guided tour options are available from the Wanaka waterfront. The calm morning conditions are ideal, with water clarity revealing the lake bed metres below.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the shores of Lake Wanaka, where crystal-clear water stretches toward snow-dusted mountains in every direction. Standing on a paddleboard here is like walking on glass — the water is so clear you can see the stones on the lake bed five metres below your feet. Paddle out toward the famous That Wanaka Tree, a lone willow that has become one of New Zealand's most photographed sights. Continue further and you'll reach Ruby Island, a nature reserve just offshore with native bush and birdsong. The mornings are best, when the lake is often perfectly flat and the mountains reflect on the surface like a mirror. It's equal parts exercise, meditation, and natural wonder.

reviews.summary: The Stand-Up Paddleboarding on Lake Wanaka adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Glide across Lake Wanaka's pristine waters on a stand-up paddleboard. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Clutha River Jet Boat & Fishing
pois.short: Jet boat New Zealand's highest-volume river, the mighty Clutha.
pois.commentary: The Clutha River is New Zealand's highest-volume river and second longest. Jet boat tours explore the upper reaches near Lake Wanaka outlet and the dramatic gorges downstream. The river also supports excellent brown trout fishing, with guided trips available throughout the season.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the banks of the Clutha River, the mightiest river in New Zealand by volume. More water flows through here than any other river in the country, and you're about to experience its power firsthand from a jet boat. The Clutha carves through dramatic gorges where sheer rock walls tower above the blue-green water, then opens into braided channels where your driver will demonstrate the full agility of the jet boat with 360-degree spins and high-speed turns. This river was the heart of Central Otago's gold rush in the 1860s, and you'll pass old mining sites where fortunes were made and lost. The Clutha is also home to some of New Zealand's finest brown trout — if you spot one, consider coming back with a rod.

reviews.summary: The Clutha River Jet Boat & Fishing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Jet boat New Zealand's highest-volume river, the mighty Clutha. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Otago Central Rail Trail
pois.short: Cycle 152 km along a historic rail trail through Central Otago's golden landscapes.
pois.commentary: The Otago Central Rail Trail follows 152 km of disused railway between Middlemarch and Clyde. The trail passes through tunnels, over viaducts, and across the tussock-covered Maniototo Plain. It's one of New Zealand's Great Rides and can be completed in 3-5 days or sampled on day rides.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at one end of New Zealand's original Great Ride — the Otago Central Rail Trail. This 152-kilometre trail follows the old Otago Central railway line through some of the South Island's most distinctive and hauntingly beautiful landscapes. You'll cycle through hand-hewn tunnels, across towering viaducts, and along the vast golden plains of the Maniototo, where the big Central Otago sky stretches forever. The grade never exceeds one in fifty — it was built for trains, after all — so it's gentle riding through terrain that changes from gorge to farmland to semi-arid steppe. Along the way, tiny heritage towns like Wedderburn, Ranfurly, and Naseby offer refreshments, accommodation, and a genuine taste of rural New Zealand life. Allow three to five days for the full trail, or pick a day section.

reviews.summary: The Otago Central Rail Trail adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Cycle 152 km along a historic rail trail through Central Otago's golden landscapes. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Moeraki Boulders Kayak Tour
pois.short: Kayak past the mysterious Moeraki Boulders and along the Otago coast.
pois.commentary: Kayaking from Moeraki offers a unique perspective on the famous spherical boulders and the rugged Otago coastline. Tours often include encounters with fur seals, blue penguins, and seabirds. The boulders formed over millions of years on the ancient seabed through a process called concretion.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Moeraki, home to one of New Zealand's most mysterious geological formations. The Moeraki Boulders are enormous spherical rocks scattered along the beach, some over two metres in diameter and weighing several tonnes. From your kayak, you'll see them from an angle most visitors never experience — framed by the waves, with the wild Otago coastline stretching behind them. Scientists say the boulders formed over four million years ago through a process called concretion on the ancient seabed. Maori legend tells a different story — they're gourds washed ashore from the wreck of the great canoe Araiteuru. As you paddle along the coast, watch for New Zealand fur seals basking on rocks and little blue penguins bobbing in the waves.

reviews.summary: The Moeraki Boulders Kayak Tour adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak past the mysterious Moeraki Boulders and along the Otago coast. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Otago Peninsula Wildlife Cruise
pois.short: Cruise the Otago Peninsula to see albatross, seals, and rare penguins.
pois.commentary: Wildlife cruises from Wellers Rock explore Otago Harbour and the exposed coastline of the Otago Peninsula. The area is home to the world's only mainland breeding colony of royal albatross, plus New Zealand fur seals, blue penguins, and yellow-eyed penguins. Guided tours run year-round.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the gateway to one of the world's most remarkable wildlife experiences. The Otago Peninsula is home to the only mainland breeding colony of royal albatross on earth — these magnificent birds have wingspans of over three metres and can fly for years without touching land. Your cruise will take you along the rugged coastline where New Zealand fur seals haul out on rocky platforms, rare yellow-eyed penguins waddle ashore at dusk, and if you're lucky, you might spot a pod of dusky dolphins riding the bow wave. The Otago Peninsula has been called the wildlife capital of New Zealand, and from the water you'll understand why. Keep your camera ready — the albatross often soar directly over the boat.

reviews.summary: The Otago Peninsula Wildlife Cruise adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Cruise the Otago Peninsula to see albatross, seals, and rare penguins. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Cromwell Goldfields ATV Adventure
pois.short: ATV through historic gold mining country in the Cromwell Gorge.
pois.commentary: ATV tours in the Cromwell area explore the historic goldfields of Central Otago, where Chinese and European miners sought fortunes in the 1860s. Routes pass through river gorges, over tussock ridgelines, and past remnants of the gold rush era including stone cottages and mine workings.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in Cromwell, the heart of Central Otago's gold mining heritage, and your ATV is about to carry you through landscapes where fortune seekers once panned for gold in every creek and river. The trails wind through the dramatic Cromwell Gorge, across tussock-covered ridgelines, and past the stone ruins of Chinese miners' cottages that have stood here since the 1860s. Central Otago has a wild, dry beauty that's unlike anywhere else in New Zealand — golden hills, schist rock outcrops, and big open skies. Your ATV handles the rough terrain with ease, splashing through creek crossings and climbing to viewpoints where you can see the turquoise waters of Lake Dunstan and the snow-capped peaks beyond. The history here is tangible — you're riding through a landscape that was once swarming with thousands of miners, and their traces are everywhere if you know where to look.

reviews.summary: The Cromwell Goldfields ATV Adventure adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. ATV through historic gold mining country in the Cromwell Gorge. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Wye Creek Ice Climbing
pois.short: Climb frozen waterfalls with ice axes near Queenstown in winter.
pois.commentary: Winter ice climbing near Queenstown and Wanaka takes place on frozen waterfalls and ice formations in accessible gullies. Guided trips provide all technical equipment including ice axes, crampons, ropes, and helmets. The season typically runs from June to September depending on freeze conditions.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the base of a frozen waterfall, and in your hands are an ice axe and crampons — the tools you'll use to climb it. Winter ice climbing in the Queenstown and Wanaka region transforms familiar waterfalls into vertical ice walls that offer a completely different kind of climbing challenge. Your guide will set up top-rope anchors and teach you the techniques — front-pointing your crampons into the ice, swinging your axes to find placements, and trusting the friction of steel on frozen water. The ice is a living material, groaning and creaking as you climb, and its colours range from clear glass to deep blue. No previous climbing experience is necessary — the guides are expert instructors and the roped systems keep you safe. But make no mistake, pulling yourself up a frozen waterfall is physically demanding and genuinely thrilling. The winter landscapes around you are stunning — snow-covered peaks, frozen creeks, and the crisp silence of the alpine zone.

reviews.summary: The Wye Creek Ice Climbing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Climb frozen waterfalls with ice axes near Queenstown in winter. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Gibbston Valley Zipline
pois.short: Zipline through the Gibbston Valley wine region above the Kawarau River.
pois.commentary: The Gibbston Valley zipline experience features a series of lines above the Kawarau River gorge in the heart of the Gibbston wine region. Lines reach heights of 60 metres above the valley floor with views of vineyards, the river, and surrounding mountains. It combines adventure with the area's wine heritage.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in the Gibbston Valley, known as the Valley of the Vines and home to some of Central Otago's finest pinot noir — and now, a zipline experience that lets you fly above it all. Your series of ziplines will carry you up to 60 metres above the Kawarau River gorge, soaring past schist rock cliffs and over the vineyard terraces that produce some of New Zealand's most celebrated wines. Each line offers a different perspective on this dramatic landscape, from close-up views of the gorge walls to wide panoramas of the valley and surrounding mountains. Between lines, your guides share the geological and viticultural stories of the region — how ancient glaciers carved this gorge, and how the unique microclimate creates perfect conditions for world-class pinot noir. It's the rare adventure that feeds both your adrenaline and your appreciation for fine wine.

reviews.summary: The Gibbston Valley Zipline adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Zipline through the Gibbston Valley wine region above the Kawarau River. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Mt Difficulty Wines
pois.short: Bannockburn icon perched above Cromwell Basin — outstanding Pinot Noir and a destination restaurant.
pois.commentary: Mt Difficulty's cellar door sits on a rocky outcrop high above the Cromwell Basin with sweeping views to Lake Dunstan. The Pinot Noir is the star — concentrated and complex, reflecting Bannockburn's schist soils and extreme climate. The restaurant serves sustainable dishes (Lumina lamb, Canter Valley duck) that match the wines perfectly. The Long Gully vineyard tour is worth booking for the deeper story.

reviews.summary: The views and wine quality earn consistent five stars. The restaurant is called Central Otago's best. Some find the drive to Bannockburn from Queenstown longer than expected, but all agree it's worth it.

pois.name: Felton Road Wines
pois.short: New Zealand's most celebrated Pinot Noir — biodynamic Bannockburn estate with appointment-only tastings.
pois.commentary: Felton Road is arguably New Zealand's greatest Pinot Noir producer. The biodynamic Bannockburn estate crafts wines of extraordinary depth — Block 3 and Block 5 Pinot Noirs are sought after worldwide. Tastings are by appointment only, keeping the experience intimate and educational. Winemaker Blair Walter's commitment to biodynamics and terroir expression has made this a pilgrimage site for serious wine lovers.

reviews.summary: Wine critics consistently rate Felton Road among the world's finest Pinot producers. The biodynamic approach impresses. By-appointment only means you need to plan, but every visitor describes it as unmissable.

pois.name: Carrick Winery & Restaurant
pois.short: Organic Bannockburn winery with a superb restaurant beside the inlet — local game and estate wines.
pois.commentary: Carrick's certified organic vineyard sits beside the Bannockburn Inlet, and the restaurant makes the most of the waterside setting. The menu leans into Central Otago's larder — local game, high-country lamb, seasonal vegetables — all paired with estate wines. The Pinot Noir and Riesling are standouts. The relaxed, unpretentious atmosphere is a refreshing contrast to some of the region's more polished operations.

reviews.summary: The lakeside restaurant setting is frequently called magical. The organic wines impress, especially the Riesling. Some note service can be slow when busy, but the food quality compensates.

pois.name: Te Kano Estate
pois.short: Award-winning architectural cellar door above Lake Dunstan — single-vineyard wines and gourmet deli platters.
pois.commentary: Te Kano's cellar door is an architectural showpiece — built into the slopes above Lake Dunstan, it mirrors the rugged landscape with schist stone and dramatic angles. The single-vineyard Pinot Noirs are precise and site-specific, and the mini deli creates custom platters to enjoy on the lawn or inside. It's one of Central Otago's newest wineries but already among its most impressive.

reviews.summary: The architecture alone is worth the visit, reviewers say. The wines show real promise for a young estate. Some note prices reflect the premium positioning, but the experience justifies it.

pois.name: Terra Sancta
pois.short: Historic Bannockburn vineyard on gold-mining land — concentrated Pinot Noir from old vines.
pois.commentary: Terra Sancta's vineyards sit on former gold-mining land in Bannockburn, and the schist-rich soils give the wines a distinctive mineral character. The Mysterious Diggings Pinot Noir is their flagship — dark, concentrated and age-worthy. The cellar door in the old Bannockburn settlement has a heritage charm that connects you to the region's gold-rush past. Small-batch production means wines can sell out.

reviews.summary: Wine enthusiasts praise the terroir-driven Pinot Noirs. The gold-mining heritage adds atmosphere. Some find the cellar door modest, but the wines speak volumes.

pois.name: Cromwell Heritage Precinct
pois.short: Restored gold-rush buildings beside Lake Dunstan — cafés, artisan food shops and the gateway to wine country.
pois.commentary: When the Clyde Dam raised Lake Dunstan in the 1990s, Cromwell's historic precinct was relocated and restored. Today it's a charming collection of stone buildings housing cafés, artisan food producers and the Pinot Junction wine information centre. It's the natural starting point for a Bannockburn wine trail — grab a coffee, get recommendations from the locals, then cycle or drive to the cellar doors.

reviews.summary: Visitors enjoy the heritage atmosphere and lakeside setting. The cafés are praised for good coffee and local food. Some find it touristy, but most appreciate the convenient wine-trail starting point.

pois.name: Domain Road Vineyard
pois.short: Small Bannockburn producer with concentrated single-vineyard Pinot Noir and Riesling.
pois.commentary: Domain Road is a small family operation in Bannockburn producing just a few thousand cases annually. The focus is on Pinot Noir and Riesling from their own vineyard, and the wines show remarkable intensity for their modest production. The cellar door is unpretentious — you'll likely taste with the winemaker. The Defiance Pinot Noir is their top cuvée and worth seeking out.

reviews.summary: Small-producer fans love the personal touch and wine quality. The Riesling is called an underrated gem. Limited opening hours mean checking ahead is essential.

pois.name: Akarua Wines & Kitchen
pois.short: Bannockburn estate with a cellar door kitchen and one of Central Otago's best méthode traditionnelle sparkling wines.
pois.commentary: Akarua combines a smart cellar door with a kitchen serving local produce — platters, seasonal dishes, and excellent coffee. Their méthode traditionnelle sparkling (Brut NV and Rosé) is among Central Otago's finest, and the Pinot Noir from the Bannockburn and Pisa vineyards shows real depth. The setting is beautiful — vines stretch away toward the Kawarau Gorge in every direction.

reviews.summary: The sparkling wines surprise and delight visitors. The kitchen serves excellent seasonal food. Some note the cellar door can get busy on summer weekends.

pois.name: Clyde Village Food Trail
pois.short: Historic gold-rush village with excellent cafés, artisan producers and the gateway to the Otago Rail Trail.
pois.commentary: Clyde is a beautifully preserved gold-rush village at the foot of the Clyde Dam, and its food scene punches well above its weight. Paulina's, Olivers Restaurant (in the 1863 stone hotel) and the Post Office Café are all excellent. The village is surrounded by stone-fruit orchards — cherries, apricots and nectarines in summer — and is the eastern terminus of the Otago Central Rail Trail.

reviews.summary: The heritage village charm combined with quality food wins visitors over. Olivers Restaurant in the old hotel is a favourite. The stone-fruit season (December-February) is the best time to visit.

pois.name: Desert Heart
pois.short: Tiny Bannockburn producer making bold, concentrated Pinot Noir from one of the driest sites in Central Otago.
pois.commentary: Desert Heart takes its name from Bannockburn's semi-arid climate — this is one of the driest and most extreme vineyard sites in New Zealand. The wines reflect that intensity: the Pinot Noir is dark, concentrated and full of schist-derived mineral character. Production is tiny, so visiting the cellar door is often the only way to try the wines. A must for anyone serious about Central Otago Pinot.

reviews.summary: Pinot enthusiasts call this a hidden gem. The wines are intense and site-specific. Very small operation means limited hours and availability.

pois.name: Cromwell Farmers' Market
pois.short: Sunday morning market beside Lake Dunstan — Central Otago stone fruit, artisan foods and local crafts.
pois.commentary: Cromwell sits in the heart of Central Otago's stone-fruit country, and the Sunday farmers' market showcases the best of the region. In summer expect cherries, apricots and nectarines fresh from the orchards; year-round you'll find artisan cheeses, local honey, preserves and baked goods. The lakeside setting makes it a pleasant morning outing before hitting the wine trail.

reviews.summary: The stone fruit in summer is exceptional. Good variety of artisan producers year-round. Smaller than some city markets but the quality and setting compensate.

pois.name: Gibbston Valley Cheese
pois.short: Artisan cheesery in Gibbston Valley — handmade Central Otago cheeses and tastings.
pois.commentary: Gibbston Valley Cheese makes a range of handcrafted cheeses using local milk, from creamy brie to aged cheddar and blue varieties. The tasting counter in the Gibbston Valley complex lets you sample the range, and the cheeses pair perfectly with wines from the neighbouring cellar doors. Pick up a selection for a vineyard picnic — you won't find these cheeses outside the region.

reviews.summary: Cheese lovers praise the quality and local sourcing. The tastings are generous. Some note the range is smaller than expected, but the quality is consistently high.

pois.name: Rippon Vineyard
pois.short: Biodynamic Wanaka vineyard with the most photographed setting in NZ wine — Lake Wanaka and mountain views.
pois.commentary: Rippon may have the most beautiful vineyard location in the world — the vines cascade down to the shore of Lake Wanaka with snow-capped mountains rising behind. The biodynamic wines (especially the Mature Vine Pinot Noir and the Emma Pinot Noir) are world-class, and the cellar door experience is profoundly connected to place. Linden Estate, now in its second generation under Nick Mills, continues to push boundaries.

reviews.summary: Possibly the most beautiful vineyard in the world. The wines match the setting — world-class Pinot Noir. By appointment in peak season; the drive along the lake is stunning.

pois.name: Burn Cottage Vineyard
pois.short: Biodynamic Cromwell vineyard — meticulous winemaking and one of Central Otago's most compelling Pinot Noirs.
pois.commentary: Burn Cottage takes biodynamics seriously — the vineyard is farmed as a complete ecosystem, with sheep, bees and native plantings alongside the vines. The result is Pinot Noir of extraordinary depth and elegance. The cellar door is by appointment, ensuring an unhurried, educational experience with the winemaking team. The Moonlight Race Pinot Noir (second label) is excellent value; the estate wine is a masterpiece.

reviews.summary: One of Central Otago's most thoughtful producers. The biodynamic commitment is genuine and the wines reflect it. By-appointment model means planning ahead.

pois.name: Wanaka Sunday Market
pois.short: Lakeside Sunday market in Wanaka — Central Otago produce, artisan food and mountain views.
pois.commentary: Wanaka's Sunday market runs along the lakefront from October to Easter, showcasing Central Otago's best produce. Stone fruit in summer is the highlight, alongside artisan cheeses, local honey, baked goods and street food. The lakefront location with views to the mountains makes it a quintessential Wanaka morning. Combine with a coffee at one of Wanaka's excellent cafés for the full experience.

reviews.summary: Beautiful setting with quality produce. The summer stone fruit is exceptional. Seasonal hours — not running in winter.

pois.name: Valli Vineyards
pois.short: Grant Taylor's single-vineyard Pinot Noirs from Bannockburn, Gibbston and Waitaki — taste the terroir difference.
pois.commentary: Grant Taylor was the founding winemaker at Gibbston Valley and has made Central Otago Pinot Noir for decades. His Valli project showcases how Pinot Noir changes across three Central Otago sub-regions — Bannockburn, Gibbston and the Waitaki Valley. Tasting the three side by side at the cellar door is one of the best wine education experiences in the region. Each wine is distinct and site-specific.

reviews.summary: The terroir-comparison tasting is revelatory. Grant Taylor's experience shows in every wine. By-appointment — book ahead.

pois.name: Fleur's Place
pois.short: Legendary Moeraki seafood restaurant — fisherman's cottage serving the freshest catch on the Otago coast.
pois.commentary: Fleur Sullivan's tiny seafood restaurant in the fishing village of Moeraki is one of New Zealand's most famous food destinations. The converted fisherman's wharf serves whatever's fresh off the boats — blue cod, crayfish, mussels, oysters — with minimal fuss and maximum flavour. The setting, overlooking the harbour with fishing boats bobbing nearby, is perfect. Booking is essential; it's small and always full.

reviews.summary: The freshest seafood in NZ, according to many. The harbour setting is magical. Very small — booking well ahead is essential.

pois.name: Coal Pit Wines
pois.short: Tiny Gibbston producer making concentrated, single-vineyard Pinot Noir from some of the highest vines in NZ.
pois.commentary: Coal Pit's vineyard sits at over 400 metres in the Gibbston Valley — among the highest in New Zealand — and the extreme altitude shows in wines of remarkable concentration and finesse. The Pinot Noir and Pinot Gris are both excellent, and the tiny production means most wine sells at the cellar door. Tastings are available through Kinross in Gibbston, where Coal Pit is one of the featured producers.

reviews.summary: Small-producer Pinot Noir at its best. The high-altitude story adds interest. Available through Kinross cellar door rather than its own dedicated space.

pois.name: Royal Albatross Centre — Taiaroa Head
pois.short: The world's only mainland royal albatross breeding colony — 3-metre wingspan birds nesting at Taiaroa Head.
pois.commentary: Taiaroa Head on the Otago Peninsula hosts the world's only mainland breeding colony of northern royal albatross. With a wingspan of over 3 metres, these birds are extraordinary to watch. The Royal Albatross Centre offers guided tours with viewing hide access during breeding season.

reviews.summary: World-class wildlife experience. The albatross are magnificent. Guided tour essential. Book ahead.

pois.name: Penguin Place — Yellow-Eyed Penguin Reserve
pois.short: Private conservation reserve protecting NZ's most endangered penguin — approach via unique trench-hide system.
pois.commentary: Penguin Place on the Otago Peninsula is a private conservation reserve for hoiho (yellow-eyed penguin), the world's rarest penguin. An innovative trench-and-hide system allows visitors to approach nesting birds without disturbance. Conservation levy funds habitat restoration and predator control.

reviews.summary: Unique penguin viewing experience. The trench system is clever and non-intrusive. Conservation funding model is excellent.

pois.name: Pilots Beach — Blue Penguin Colony
pois.short: Watch little blue penguins waddle ashore at dusk at the base of Taiaroa Head.
pois.commentary: Pilots Beach at the base of Taiaroa Head on the Otago Peninsula hosts a monitored little blue penguin colony. Guided evening viewing from a grandstand allows visitors to watch penguins come ashore after dark. Numbers peak during breeding season.

reviews.summary: Reliable penguin viewing. The grandstand setup is well managed. Arrive early for best seats.

pois.name: Sandfly Bay — NZ Sea Lion Colony
pois.short: NZ sea lions haul out on this wild Otago Peninsula beach — one of the rarest pinnipeds on earth.
pois.commentary: Sandfly Bay on the Otago Peninsula is a regular haul-out for NZ sea lions, one of the world's rarest pinnipeds with fewer than 10,000 remaining. Yellow-eyed penguins also nest in the dunes. Access via a steep sandy track. Maintain a 10-metre distance from all wildlife.

reviews.summary: Wild and beautiful. Sea lion encounters are thrilling. Keep your distance — they can be aggressive.

pois.name: Moeraki — Yellow-Eyed Penguin Viewing
pois.short: Yellow-eyed penguins nest near the famous Moeraki Boulders — watch them come ashore at Katiki Point.
pois.commentary: Katiki Point near the Moeraki Boulders hosts a DOC-managed yellow-eyed penguin viewing hide. Late afternoon is the best time to see these endangered birds returning from the sea. NZ fur seals also haul out on the rocks below the lighthouse. Combine with a Moeraki Boulders visit.

reviews.summary: Great combination with the boulders. Penguin viewing requires patience. The DOC hide is well positioned.

pois.name: Sinclair Wetlands — Birding Paradise
pois.short: 1,200 hectares of Otago wetland — bittern, fernbird, marsh crake, and abundant waterfowl.
pois.commentary: Sinclair Wetlands between Dunedin and Balclutha is one of the South Island's most significant remaining wetlands. Over 70 bird species have been recorded, including Australasian bittern, fernbird, marsh crake, and abundant waterfowl. Guided canoe tours available.

reviews.summary: Outstanding wetland birding. The canoe tour is the best way to experience it. Book ahead.

pois.name: Oamaru Blue Penguin Colony
pois.short: NZ's largest accessible little blue penguin colony — watch hundreds waddle ashore each evening.
pois.commentary: The Oamaru Blue Penguin Colony is the largest easily accessible kororā colony in NZ, with over 200 birds returning to nest each evening during peak season. Grandstand and premium viewing options available. Daytime nest-box viewing and backstage tours also offered.

reviews.summary: Fantastic penguin experience. The numbers are impressive. Premium seating is worth the upgrade.

pois.name: Hoopers Inlet — Wading Bird Estuary
pois.short: Sheltered Otago Peninsula inlet with excellent shorebird viewing — godwit, spoonbill, and oystercatcher.
pois.commentary: Hoopers Inlet on the Otago Peninsula is a productive tidal estuary supporting diverse wading birds. Bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, variable oystercatcher, and pied stilt are regular visitors. The inlet is easily viewed from the road and several pull-off points.

reviews.summary: Easy roadside birding. Good variety of wading birds. Low tide is best. Combine with peninsula wildlife loop.

pois.name: Aramoana Wildlife Refuge
pois.short: Harbour mouth refuge with diverse birdlife — NZ dotterel, royal spoonbill, and Stewart Island shag.
pois.commentary: Aramoana at the entrance to Otago Harbour is a designated wildlife management area. The salt marsh, mudflats, and rocky shore support diverse birdlife including NZ dotterel, royal spoonbill, and the southernmost Stewart Island shag colony. Walk the mole for pelagic seabird viewing.

reviews.summary: Good variety of habitats and birds in a small area. The mole walk is great for seabirds. Can be windy.

pois.name: Macraes Flat — Grand & Otago Skink Habitat
pois.short: Last stronghold of the critically endangered grand skink and Otago skink — unique NZ lizards.
pois.commentary: Macraes Flat in inland Otago is the last significant population centre for the grand skink and Otago skink, both critically endangered. DOC runs an intensive predator control programme. The skinks bask on rock tors and are best seen on warm, sunny days. Guided tours available.

reviews.summary: Unique reptile viewing. Warm, sunny weather essential. The landscape is stark and beautiful. DOC can advise on access.

pois.name: Shag Point — Fur Seal & Yellow-Eyed Penguin
pois.short: Rocky headland with NZ fur seals and yellow-eyed penguin nesting — free roadside wildlife.
pois.commentary: Shag Point between Oamaru and Dunedin hosts NZ fur seals on the rocks and yellow-eyed penguins nesting in the vegetation above. A short walk from the car park leads to viewing platforms. NZ sea lions occasionally visit. Free and easily accessible.

reviews.summary: Easy free wildlife viewing. Great variety of species. Short detour off SH1 well worth it.

pois.name: Waikouaiti Estuary — Wetland Walk
pois.short: Coastal estuary north of Dunedin with white-faced heron, pied stilt, and seasonal godwit.
pois.commentary: The Waikouaiti Estuary provides a pleasant wetland walk with diverse wading bird viewing. White-faced heron, pied stilt, NZ shoveler, and seasonal bar-tailed godwit frequent the area. The walking track follows the estuary edge through coastal scrub.

reviews.summary: Quiet and uncrowded. Good birding at low tide. Easy walk suitable for families.

pois.name: Sutton Salt Lake — Unique Ecology
pois.short: NZ's only inland salt lake — unique ecosystem supporting species found nowhere else.
pois.commentary: Sutton Salt Lake near Middlemarch is NZ's only naturally occurring inland saline lake. The lake and surrounding tussock support a unique community of salt-tolerant plants and invertebrates found nowhere else in the country. The landscape is stark Central Otago at its most atmospheric.

reviews.summary: Unique geological feature. The surrounding tussock landscape is beautiful. Best combined with Taieri Gorge trip.

pois.name: Katiki Point Lighthouse — Penguin & Seal Viewing
pois.short: Historic lighthouse headland with yellow-eyed penguins, fur seals, and breeding NZ fur seals.
pois.commentary: Katiki Point lighthouse near Moeraki overlooks a rocky coast where NZ fur seals haul out and yellow-eyed penguins nest. A DOC viewing hide provides close penguin viewing without disturbance. The lighthouse adds historical interest. Free access from the car park.

reviews.summary: Excellent free wildlife viewing. The hide is well positioned. Late afternoon best for penguins.

pois.name: Bushy Beach — Yellow-Eyed Penguin Hide
pois.short: DOC viewing hide overlooking one of Oamaru's yellow-eyed penguin nesting beaches.
pois.commentary: Bushy Beach south of Oamaru has a DOC viewing hide overlooking the sandy beach where yellow-eyed penguins come ashore in late afternoon. The hide allows viewing without disturbing the birds. NZ fur seals sometimes rest on the rocks at the south end.

reviews.summary: Good free penguin viewing from the hide. Late afternoon is essential. Don't walk on the beach during penguin time.

pois.name: Dunedin Public Art Gallery
pois.short: New Zealand's oldest public art gallery with a superb collection including rare Japanese prints and NZ masterworks.
pois.commentary: The Dunedin Public Art Gallery is New Zealand's oldest public gallery, established in 1884, and holds one of the country's most significant art collections. The gallery is particularly renowned for its outstanding collection of Japanese prints and its comprehensive holdings of New Zealand art from the colonial period to the present. Major works by Frances Hodgkins (a Dunedin native), Colin McCahon, Ralph Hotere, and other important New Zealand artists feature prominently. The gallery occupies a modern building in the Octagon, Dunedin's central plaza, and presents an ambitious programme of temporary exhibitions alongside permanent collection displays. Admission is free. The gallery has a strong international collection for its size, reflecting Dunedin's historic wealth from the gold rush era. The ground-floor café is a popular meeting spot. Allow at least 1.5 hours for the permanent collection and current exhibitions. The gallery shop is excellent for art books and prints.

reviews.summary: A genuinely impressive gallery for a city this size — the Japanese print collection is world-class. Free entry is generous given the quality. The Octagon location makes it easy to combine with other Dunedin attractions.

pois.name: Dunedin Street Art Trail
pois.short: World-renowned collection of large-scale murals by international and local artists throughout central Dunedin.
pois.commentary: Dunedin's street art scene has gained international recognition through a programme that invites world-class artists to create large-scale murals on buildings throughout the central city. Begun around 2014, the project has transformed Dunedin's laneways and walls into an extraordinary open-air gallery. Artists including Phlegm (UK), Pixel Pancho (Italy), DALeast (China), and New Zealand artists Askew One and Elliot O'Donnell have contributed stunning works. The murals range from hyper-realistic portraits to fantastical creatures and abstract designs, many reflecting Dunedin's unique character, wildlife, and history. A self-guided walking trail map is available from the i-SITE or online, covering about 30 major works in the central city area. The trail takes approximately 1.5 to 2 hours and reveals hidden laneways and streets you would otherwise miss. New works are added regularly. The best concentration is in the warehouse district around Bath Street and Vogel Street. Free to explore at any time.

reviews.summary: Absolutely incredible street art — some of the best we've seen anywhere in the world. The self-guided trail takes you to parts of Dunedin you'd never otherwise discover. A real highlight of visiting the city.

pois.name: Regent Theatre Dunedin
pois.short: Grand heritage theatre in the heart of Dunedin, combining touring performances with a boutique cinema experience.
pois.commentary: The Regent Theatre occupies a commanding position on the Octagon in central Dunedin, a grand heritage building that has been entertaining audiences since 1928. Originally built as a picture palace, the theatre features an ornate interior with classical architectural detailing, a sweeping staircase, and the atmospheric ambience of golden-age cinema. Today it operates as both a performing arts venue and a boutique cinema, hosting touring theatre productions, concerts, comedy shows, and first-run films. The main auditorium seats approximately 1,600. The theatre's dual role means you can experience its beautiful interior whether attending a major show or simply catching a movie. Located at the heart of Dunedin's entertainment precinct in the Octagon, it is surrounded by excellent restaurants and bars. The building underwent heritage restoration to preserve its 1920s grandeur while adding modern amenities. Check the programme for current shows and film screenings.

reviews.summary: What a beautiful theatre — the grand interior makes any show feel special. Even watching a regular movie here is a treat. One of the most elegant venues in the South Island.

pois.name: Oamaru Victorian Precinct
pois.short: Extraordinarily preserved Victorian streetscape with limestone buildings, artisan studios, and steampunk culture.
pois.commentary: The Oamaru Victorian Precinct is one of New Zealand's most remarkable heritage streetscapes — a complete district of ornate Victorian commercial buildings in cream-coloured local limestone (Oamaru stone), preserved in a virtually intact state. The precinct along Harbour and Tyne Streets was the commercial heart of 1880s Oamaru, when the town was one of New Zealand's wealthiest thanks to the frozen meat trade. After decades of decline, the district was saved from demolition and revitalised as a creative quarter. Today the historic buildings house artisan studios, vintage shops, bookstores, galleries, and the Steampunk HQ museum, giving the precinct an eccentric, creative energy. Events include the Victorian Heritage Celebrations (November), Steampunk NZ Festival, and Saturday farmers market. Many buildings are accessible and free to wander. The Oamaru stone is uniquely carvable when fresh, allowing the extraordinary classical detailing that makes these buildings so special. Allow two hours to explore properly.

reviews.summary: Like stepping back in time — the Victorian buildings are incredibly beautiful and well preserved. The steampunk additions add quirky fun. One of the most unique heritage experiences in New Zealand.

pois.name: St Clair Beach Dunedin
pois.short: Dunedin's iconic surf beach with a heated saltwater pool, seaside esplanade, and excellent cafés.
pois.commentary: St Clair Beach is Dunedin's most popular and characterful beach, a wide sweep of sand facing the open Pacific on the city's southern coast. The beach is renowned for its surf — consistent swells attract boardriders year-round, and the brave surf in winter when the Southern Ocean delivers powerful waves. The heated St Clair Salt Water Pool on the esplanade is a Dunedin institution, allowing outdoor swimming even when air temperatures are bracing. The rebuilt esplanade features excellent cafés and restaurants with ocean views. St Clair adjoins St Kilda Beach, together forming a long continuous stretch of sand. The beach is patrolled by surf lifeguards during summer. The area has a distinct bohemian character with independent shops and a strong local community feel. On a sunny day, the café-lined esplanade rivals any beach promenade in the country. The beach can be wild and dramatic in southerly weather — equally beautiful in its own way. Located just 10 minutes from central Dunedin.

reviews.summary: Great surf beach with real character — the hot pool and esplanade cafés are brilliant. Even on a grey day there's atmosphere. One of the most underrated beach experiences in New Zealand.

pois.name: Roys Peak Track
pois.short: Iconic Wānaka day hike with one of NZ's most photographed summit viewpoints over lake and mountains.
pois.commentary: Roys Peak Track is one of New Zealand's most popular and rewarding day hikes, climbing from the shores of Lake Wānaka to a 1,578-metre summit with arguably the most photographed viewpoint in the country. The track ascends steadily through open tussock farmland, gaining approximately 1,200 metres of elevation over about 6 kilometres each way. While physically demanding, the track is well-formed and requires no technical ability. The famous viewpoint at the ridgeline spur (about 80% of the way up) provides the iconic Instagram vista of Lake Wānaka far below, with Mount Aspiring and the Southern Alps beyond. The true summit adds even grander panoramic views. Allow 5-6 hours return. Start early to avoid the heat and crowds — the track is fully exposed with no shade. Carry plenty of water and sun protection. The track is closed during lambing season (October-November). Winter snow may require appropriate gear. The car park on the Wānaka-Glendhu Bay road fills quickly on summer mornings.

reviews.summary: The views are absolutely breathtaking — worth every step of the climb. Start early to beat the crowds and the heat. Bring lots of water and sunscreen as there's no shade at all.

pois.name: Katiki Point Lighthouse & Wildlife
pois.short: Coastal headland near Moeraki with a historic lighthouse, yellow-eyed penguins, and fur seal colony.
pois.commentary: Katiki Point is a windswept headland just south of the famous Moeraki Boulders, offering one of the Otago coast's best wildlife viewing experiences. A short walk from the car park leads to the 1878 lighthouse, now automated, with views along the coast in both directions. The headland is home to a breeding colony of hoiho (yellow-eyed penguins), one of the world's rarest penguin species, best viewed at dawn or dusk when they traverse the beach. New Zealand fur seals haul out on the rocks below the cliffs, and the point is a habitat for little blue penguins and various seabirds. Viewing hides allow observation without disturbing the wildlife. The Katiki Point Historic Reserve is managed by DOC and local conservation volunteers who monitor the penguin population. Visit quietly and keep distance from all wildlife, particularly during breeding season.

reviews.summary: An excellent wildlife viewing spot often overlooked by visitors rushing to the Moeraki Boulders. Seeing yellow-eyed penguins in their natural habitat is special. The lighthouse and coastal views add to the experience.

pois.name: Haast Pass - Wanaka Road
pois.short: Dramatic alpine highway crossing the Southern Alps between the West Coast rainforest and the Wanaka lakes district.
pois.commentary: The Haast Pass highway (SH6) is one of New Zealand's most dramatic road journeys, connecting the lush West Coast rainforest with the dry, golden landscapes of Central Otago. The 145km route between Haast and Wanaka climbs through the Haast River valley, ancient podocarp rainforest, and across the pass at 563 metres before descending alongside the brilliant blue waters of Lakes Wanaka and Hawea. Essential stops include Thunder Creek Falls, a 28-metre waterfall accessed by a short bush walk; the Blue Pools, where transparent glacial water reveals every stone on the riverbed; and the Gates of Haast, where the river forces through a narrow rock gorge. The road is well-sealed but narrow and winding in places. Allow at least three hours with stops. Winter driving may require chains. The transition between west and east landscapes is remarkably abrupt and visually stunning.

reviews.summary: One of the South Island's most spectacular drives with dramatic landscape changes. Blue Pools and Thunder Creek Falls are must-stop highlights. The contrast from rainforest to tussock is remarkable.

pois.name: Central Otago Pinot Noir Trail
pois.short: Self-drive wine trail through the world's southernmost wine region, renowned for exceptional Pinot Noir.
pois.commentary: Central Otago is the world's southernmost major wine region and has earned an international reputation for producing Pinot Noir of exceptional quality. The wine trail stretches across several sub-regions including Bannockburn, Cromwell Basin, Bendigo, and Pisa Range, each contributing distinct character to their wines. Notable cellar doors open for tasting include Felton Road, Mt Difficulty, Burn Cottage, and Quartz Reef, among many others. The dramatic landscape of schist rock, golden tussock, and turquoise river gorges provides a stunning backdrop for wine touring. The continental climate with hot summers and cold winters creates ideal conditions for Pinot Noir, producing wines with intensity, complexity, and silky texture. Many cellar doors offer tastings by appointment only, so plan ahead. The Cromwell Heritage Precinct makes a good central base with cafes and the historic old town centre by Lake Dunstan.

reviews.summary: World-class Pinot Noir in a breathtaking landscape. The cellar door experiences are intimate and personal compared to larger wine regions. Pre-booking is essential at many wineries.

pois.name: Cardrona Distillery
pois.short: Award-winning craft distillery in the Cardrona Valley producing single malt whisky, gin, and vodka.
pois.commentary: The Cardrona Distillery is one of New Zealand's premier craft distilleries, producing single malt whisky, gin, vodka, and other spirits from a striking facility in the historic Cardrona Valley between Queenstown and Wanaka. Founded in 2015, the distillery uses traditional copper pot stills and locally sourced ingredients to create spirits of exceptional quality. The tasting experience includes flights across the spirit range, with the single malt whisky and the award-winning gin being particular highlights. The distillery building itself is architecturally impressive, designed to reference the area's gold-mining heritage. The on-site Museum of Drink explores humanity's relationship with alcohol through the ages with quirky and informative displays. The Cardrona Valley location, surrounded by tussock-covered mountains, adds grandeur to the visit. Located on the Crown Range road, it makes a natural stop when driving between Queenstown and Wanaka.

reviews.summary: A world-class distillery experience with outstanding spirits and a fascinating museum. The whisky and gin are both exceptional. The Cardrona Valley setting is spectacular.

pois.name: Moeraki Boulders
pois.short: Mysterious spherical boulders on Koekohe Beach, formed over millions of years and steeped in Maori legend.
pois.commentary: The Moeraki Boulders are large spherical concretions scattered along Koekohe Beach on the Otago coast, some reaching over two metres in diameter and weighing several tonnes. Geologically, they formed on the ancient seabed around 65 million years ago through a process of concretion, where minerals accumulated around a core over millions of years. In Maori legend, they are eel baskets and calabashes washed ashore from the wreck of the great voyaging canoe Araiteuru. The boulders are most photogenic at low tide when fully exposed, and the interplay of light at dawn and dusk creates dramatic photography opportunities. Some boulders have cracked open to reveal fascinating internal crystal structures. Free access is available from the beach car park, and the adjacent Moeraki Boulders cafe serves excellent food with ocean views. Located on SH1 between Oamaru and Dunedin, they are an easy and worthwhile roadside stop.

reviews.summary: A genuinely fascinating geological curiosity that must be seen in person to appreciate. Best visited at low tide for full impact. The cafe nearby is surprisingly good. A must-stop on the Otago coast drive.

pois.name: Wanaka Lavender Farm
pois.short: Picturesque lavender farm near Wanaka with mountain views, artisan products, and cafe.
pois.commentary: Wanaka Lavender Farm is a photogenic 4.5-hectare property on the outskirts of Wanaka, where rows of purple lavender stretch towards the surrounding mountains. The farm grows several lavender varieties and produces a range of artisan lavender products including essential oils, soaps, bath products, culinary lavender, honey, and ice cream. Peak flowering occurs from mid-December to late January when the farm is at its most visually stunning, though the shop and cafe operate year-round. The on-site cafe serves lavender-infused dishes and drinks including lavender scones and lavender lemonade. The farm makes for excellent photography with the lavender rows creating leading lines toward the mountain backdrop. A small entry fee provides access to the fields and garden areas. Located on SH6 just north of Wanaka, it is a pleasant stop en route to or from the Haast Pass or Cardrona Valley.

reviews.summary: A beautiful and fragrant farm visit, especially during peak lavender bloom. The products are well-made and the cafe is charming. Peak season visits are most rewarding but can be busy.

pois.name: Oamaru Blue Penguin Colony
pois.short: Evening viewing of little blue penguins returning to their nesting colony at Oamaru's historic harbour.
pois.commentary: The Oamaru Blue Penguin Colony provides one of New Zealand's most accessible and reliable penguin viewing experiences. Each evening at dusk, groups of korora (little blue penguins) emerge from the sea and waddle up the beach to their nesting boxes in the old harbour area. Tiered seating allows up to 300 visitors to watch the nightly parade, and the colony has grown to over 300 penguins thanks to careful management and predator control. General and premium viewing options are available, with premium providing closer access and smaller group sizes. Daytime visits allow viewing of nesting penguins through one-way glass and an interpretive display about penguin biology and conservation. The colony is located in Oamaru's historic precinct, near the Victorian-era harbour buildings. Photography with flash is prohibited to protect the penguins. Evening viewing times vary seasonally, with bookings recommended during peak summer. A charming and educational wildlife experience.

reviews.summary: A delightful evening penguin experience that is reliable and well-managed. Watching the penguins emerge from the sea and waddle to their nests is endlessly entertaining. Premium viewing is worth the extra cost.

pois.name: Dunedin Craft Beer Trail
pois.short: Self-guided craft beer trail through Dunedin's historic brewery district and vibrant taprooms.
pois.commentary: Dunedin has a deep brewing heritage dating back to the gold rush era, and today the city boasts a thriving craft beer scene centred on several excellent breweries and taprooms. Emerson's Brewery, one of New Zealand's pioneering craft breweries, operates a large taproom and restaurant near the harbour. New New New is an innovative small-batch brewery producing experimental styles. Noisy Brewing and Arc Brewery add to the variety. The university city's youthful energy fuels a lively pub and bar scene, with many establishments showcasing local craft beers. A self-guided trail connecting the main breweries makes for an excellent afternoon or evening in the city. Dunedin's Scottish heritage extends to its brewing culture, with some producers drawing on traditional Scottish and British ale styles. The Speight's Brewery, operating since 1876, offers heritage tours. Combine with Dunedin's excellent restaurant scene and live music venues for a memorable evening out.

reviews.summary: A rewarding craft beer trail in a city with genuine brewing heritage. Emerson's is the standout but smaller breweries offer exciting variety. The university atmosphere adds energy to the evening scene.

pois.name: Tunnel Beach Walk
pois.short: Short dramatic walk through a hand-carved tunnel to a hidden beach.
pois.commentary: Tunnel Beach is one of Dunedin's most striking natural attractions, reached via a short but steep walk from the car park. The highlight is a hand-cut tunnel carved through the cliff face in the 1870s by local politician John Cargill so his family could access the secluded beach below. Emerging from the dark tunnel onto the beach, surrounded by towering sandstone cliffs sculpted by wind and waves into dramatic arches and pillars, is a genuinely magical moment. The beach is only accessible at low tide, so check tide times before visiting. The clifftop walk also offers spectacular coastal views. The track is closed during lambing season from August to October. The sandstone formations are fragile, so stay on marked paths to protect the geological features.

reviews.summary: A unique experience emerging through the tunnel onto a dramatic hidden beach. Check tide times carefully. The cliff formations are stunning.

pois.name: Dunedin Street Art Trail
pois.short: A world-class collection of large-scale murals painted by international and local artists across Dunedin's heritage buildings and laneways.
pois.commentary: Dunedin has quietly become one of Australasia's premier street art cities. Since 2014, the Dunedin Street Art project has invited acclaimed artists from around the world to paint massive murals on the city's Victorian and Edwardian buildings. The result is a free outdoor gallery of over 30 major works, ranging from photorealistic portraits to abstract compositions. Artists including Phlegm (UK), ROA (Belgium), and local talent like Elliot Francis Stewart have contributed pieces that transform bland walls into conversation starters. A self-guided trail map is available from the i-SITE, and the works are concentrated enough to see most on a 90-minute walk through the central city.

reviews.summary: Art lovers and casual visitors alike are impressed by the quality and scale. The murals are well-distributed for a walking tour. Some pieces have been painted over for new works — check the current map. Free and always accessible.

pois.name: Dunedin Railway Station
pois.short: One of the most photographed buildings in New Zealand — a flamboyant Flemish Renaissance-style railway station from 1906, nicknamed 'Gingerbread George's' masterpiece.
pois.commentary: Dunedin Railway Station is an architectural confection of dark basalt, white Oamaru limestone, and Aberdeen granite that earned architect George Troup the affectionate nickname 'Gingerbread George.' Completed in 1906 during Dunedin's prosperous gold-era heyday, the station features a mosaic floor of nearly 750,000 Royal Doulton porcelain tiles, elaborate stained glass windows, and ornate plasterwork throughout. Once the busiest station in the country, it now serves primarily as the departure point for the Taieri Gorge Railway scenic train. The upper floors house the New Zealand Sports Hall of Fame and an art gallery. Even if you're not catching a train, the building itself is a destination.

reviews.summary: Universally praised as stunning architecture. The mosaic floor and stained glass are highlights. Great for photography. Some visitors wish more of the interior was accessible. The Taieri Gorge train departure adds to the experience.

pois.name: Otago Central Rail Trail
pois.short: New Zealand's original Great Ride — 152 kilometres of flat, car-free cycling through the golden landscapes of Central Otago, following the route of the old Otago Central Railway.
pois.commentary: The Rail Trail follows the former Otago Central Railway line from Middlemarch to Clyde, passing through tunnels, over historic bridges, and across the vast, dry tussock landscapes that make Central Otago feel like nowhere else in New Zealand. The trail is flat and well-surfaced, making it accessible to riders of moderate fitness, and the small towns along the route — Hyde, Ranfurly, Naseby, Lauder — offer character accommodation and genuine rural hospitality. Most cyclists take 3-4 days. The landscape shifts from gorge country to wide-open pastoral plains backed by the Hawkdun and Kakanui Ranges. Bike hire and luggage transfers are well-established.

reviews.summary: Consistently rated among NZ's best cycling experiences. The scenery and small-town hospitality are highlights. Not technical but long — reasonable fitness required. Summer and autumn are best; winter is very cold. Book accommodation ahead in peak season.

pois.name: Olveston Historic Home
pois.short: A magnificent Edwardian mansion preserved exactly as the Theomin family left it — one of New Zealand's finest historic homes, offering a window into privileged early-20th-century life.
pois.commentary: Olveston was built between 1904 and 1906 for David Theomin, a successful Dunedin businessman, and his wife Marie. When their daughter Dorothy died in 1966, she bequeathed the house and its entire contents to the city. As a result, Olveston is a rare time capsule — everything from the Japanese cloisonne vases to the 1920s telephone is original. The 35-room Jacobean-style mansion holds an extraordinary collection of decorative arts, including paintings, ceramics, textiles, and furniture gathered from the Theomins' travels around the world. Guided tours (the only way to visit) are intimate and fascinating, revealing the story of a cultured, well-travelled family in a colonial outpost.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently praise the guides and the completeness of the collection. The time-capsule quality is extraordinary. Tours are small and fill quickly — book in advance. Photography is limited inside.

pois.name: Blue Pools / Makarora
pois.short: Crystal-clear glacier-fed pools of astonishing blue, reached by a short walk through silver beech forest and across swing bridges in the Makarora Valley.
pois.commentary: The Blue Pools are one of those places where photographs struggle to capture what your eyes see. The Makarora River and Young River converge here, and the glacial meltwater is so pure and still that the pools appear an almost luminous shade of blue. Large brown and rainbow trout hang suspended in the current like fish in an aquarium. The walk from the highway takes about 30 minutes through beautiful silver beech forest and crosses two swing bridges — the second, over the Blue Pools themselves, is the money shot. On a warm day, brave swimmers jump in, though the water temperature rarely climbs above 8 degrees.

reviews.summary: The water colour is consistently described as unbelievable. The walk is short and easy. Swimming is possible but extremely cold. Can be very busy during peak tourist season — go early. Sand flies are fierce.

pois.name: Toitu Otago Settlers Museum
pois.short: New Zealand's oldest museum, telling the story of the people who shaped Otago — from early Maori arrivals to Scottish settlers, Chinese gold miners, and modern immigrants.
pois.commentary: Toitu (meaning 'to hold fast, to stand firm') is a beautifully presented museum that explores the human history of the Otago region. The Maori gallery traces the earliest Polynesian journeys to the southern coast, the Smith Gallery follows European settlement, and the compelling Chinese Miners gallery documents the often-harsh experiences of the thousands of Chinese men who came to the Otago goldfields. A stunning hall of historic vehicles includes vintage cars, horse-drawn carriages, and a Dunedin cable car. The museum is free, housed in a striking modern building connected to the historic 1898 bus station, and sits alongside the railway station precinct.

reviews.summary: Praised as one of the best free museums in the South Island. The transport hall and Chinese miners exhibit are highlights. Well laid out and suitable for all ages. Some say it needs more interactivity for children.

pois.name: Naseby Indoor Curling Rink & Ice Luge
pois.short: New Zealand's home of curling and the Southern Hemisphere's only dedicated ice luge track, in the charming goldfields town of Naseby.
pois.commentary: Naseby is the coldest town in New Zealand, regularly dropping below minus-10 degrees in winter, which makes it the natural home for ice sports. The indoor curling rink operates year-round and welcomes complete beginners for fun sessions — it's surprisingly addictive once you get the hang of sliding the stones. Outside, the ice luge track sends riders on small sleds down a purpose-built course at speeds up to 70 km/h. Naseby itself is a tiny, historic gold-mining town with an altitude feel, surrounded by the Naseby Forest (great for mountain biking and orienteering) and the Maniototo Plain. It's a quirky, memorable stop on any Central Otago itinerary.

reviews.summary: Both curling and the luge are great fun and accessible to beginners. The small-town atmosphere adds to the charm. Some find the facilities basic. The luge can be seasonal — check availability.

pois.name: Clay Cliffs / Omarama
pois.short: Dramatic pinnacles and ravines of eroded silt and gravel rising from the Mackenzie Basin — a geological oddity that looks more like Utah than New Zealand.
pois.commentary: The Clay Cliffs are a striking natural formation where ancient layers of gravel, silt, and clay have been uplifted by the Ostler Fault and then carved by wind and rain into sharp pinnacles, deep gullies, and narrow canyons. The result looks utterly alien in the context of the surrounding pastoral Mackenzie Basin — visitors frequently compare it to a mini-Bryce Canyon. The cliffs are on private farmland (a small honesty-box fee applies) and a short walk from the car park leads into the formations, where you can wander through narrow ravines with towering walls on either side. Best photographed in late afternoon light when the warm tones glow.

reviews.summary: Photographers love the dramatic formations and warm light. The walk-in is short and easy. Some visitors expected something larger. An honesty box fee of $5 per person applies. Worth the short detour.

pois.name: Dunedin Chinese Garden / Lan Yuan
pois.short: An authentic Chinese scholar's garden in the heart of Dunedin, commemorating the thousands of Chinese miners who came to the Otago goldfields in the 1860s.
pois.commentary: Lan Yuan — the Garden of Enlightenment — was a gift from Dunedin's sister city Shanghai, designed and largely built by Chinese artisans using traditional methods and materials. It commemorates the significant Chinese contribution to Otago's history: from the 1860s, thousands of Chinese men came to work the gold tailings of Central Otago, often facing discrimination and hardship. The garden is a tranquil enclave of pavilions, covered walkways, moon gates, a lotus pond, and carefully placed rocks, designed according to classical Chinese garden principles. It's a place for quiet contemplation and a powerful reminder of a community whose contribution was long overlooked.

reviews.summary: Visitors appreciate the tranquillity and the cultural significance. Beautiful design and craftsmanship. Some find it smaller than expected. The historical context about Chinese miners adds emotional depth. Small entry fee.

pois.name: Larnach Castle
pois.short: New Zealand's only castle — a grand Victorian folly on the Otago Peninsula with lavish interiors, award-winning gardens, and a dramatic family history of love, betrayal, and tragedy.
pois.commentary: William Larnach, a powerful banker and politician, built this extravagant castle between 1871 and 1887, sparing no expense on imported marble, Venetian glass, and the finest native timbers. The story of the Larnach family is operatic — involving multiple marriages, family feuds, rumoured affairs, and Larnach's eventual suicide in a parliamentary committee room. The castle fell into disrepair for decades before being lovingly restored by the Barker family from 1967. Today the interiors are richly furnished with period antiques, and the 35-acre grounds include award-winning gardens with panoramic views over the harbour, peninsula, and Pacific Ocean.

reviews.summary: The dramatic family history and the castle's story of decline and restoration fascinate visitors. The gardens and views are excellent. Some find the entry price steep but agree it's worthwhile. The cafe in the old ballroom is a treat.

pois.name: Tunnel Beach
pois.short: A hidden beach accessed through a hand-carved tunnel in the cliff — built by a Victorian politician for his family's private bathing — with dramatic sea arches and sandstone formations.
pois.commentary: Tunnel Beach is one of Dunedin's most extraordinary secrets. In the 1870s, politician John Cargill had a tunnel hand-carved through the sandstone cliffs so his family could reach the secluded beach below. The beach itself is a small, dramatic cove hemmed by towering cliffs and featuring natural sea arches and rock formations sculpted by the Southern Ocean. The walk from the car park descends steeply through farmland (the track can be slippery in wet weather) before reaching the narrow tunnel entrance. Inside, the hand-chiselled rock walls give way to a vast coastal amphitheatre. Swimming is dangerous due to strong currents, but the scenery is breathtaking.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe it as magical and unique. The tunnel adds a sense of adventure. The walk down is steep — not suitable for those with mobility issues. Can be closed during lambing season. Check tide times before visiting.

pois.name: St Clair Hot Salt Water Pool
pois.short: A heated outdoor saltwater pool perched on the edge of the Pacific Ocean at St Clair Beach — swim in warm water while waves crash against the sea wall metres away.
pois.commentary: St Clair Hot Salt Water Pool is one of Dunedin's most beloved institutions. Perched right on the ocean's edge, the 25-metre pool is heated to a comfortable temperature year-round, meaning you can float in warm water while watching surfers tackle the break at St Clair Beach and Southern Ocean swells crash against the sea wall just below. The setting is gloriously exposed — wind, spray, and weather are part of the experience. The pool has been operating in various forms since the early 1900s, and after a major rebuild it now includes a separate children's pool and modern changing facilities. A swim here followed by coffee at one of the St Clair Esplanade cafes is peak Dunedin.

reviews.summary: Locals and visitors love the unique ocean-edge setting. The heated water is a welcome contrast to Dunedin's climate. Can be very exposed in bad weather. Opening hours vary seasonally — check before visiting. Very affordable.

pois.name: Puzzling World / Stuart Landsborough's
pois.short: A delightfully bizarre world of optical illusions, brain teasers, and a massive outdoor maze that has been bamboozling visitors in Wanaka since 1973.
pois.commentary: Puzzling World is one of those uniquely New Zealand attractions — slightly eccentric, enormously fun, and impossible to explain to someone who hasn't been. Created by Stuart Landsborough, the complex features the famous tilting house where water appears to flow uphill, a hall of following faces, rooms of impossible geometry, and the Great Maze — 1.5 kilometres of passages with four corner towers to find. The illusion rooms are genuinely disorienting and produce hilarious photos. It's been operating since 1973 and has a wonderful retro charm alongside newer additions. Adults enjoy it just as much as children, especially the puzzle cafe where every table has brain teasers.

reviews.summary: Fun for all ages — adults are often more entertained than children. The maze is genuinely challenging. The illusion rooms make great photos. Some find the entry price high for what amounts to about 2 hours. Quirky and memorable.

pois.name: Bendigo Historic Reserve
pois.short: The atmospheric remains of an 1860s gold-mining settlement in the dry hills above Cromwell — stone cottages, mine shafts, and water races preserved in a landscape of golden tussock.
pois.commentary: Bendigo was once a thriving gold-mining settlement, but when the easily won alluvial gold ran out, the population drifted away, leaving behind an extraordinary collection of stone ruins in the dry Central Otago hills. Today the DOC-managed reserve preserves the remains of miners' cottages, a schoolhouse, the Welshman's Gully quartz workings, and an extensive network of water races that carried water to the diggings. The landscape is pure Central Otago — golden tussock, schist rock, blue sky, and silence. A well-formed loop walk of about two hours passes the main sites, with interpretive panels bringing the stories of the Chinese and European miners to life.

reviews.summary: History enthusiasts love the well-preserved ruins and the atmospheric setting. The walk is easy to moderate. Very hot in summer and exposed — bring water and sun protection. Quiet and uncrowded even in peak season.

pois.name: Steampunk HQ
pois.short: A fire-breathing locomotive, a portal to an alternate dimension, and a wonderfully eccentric art installation in Oamaru.
pois.commentary: Steampunk HQ opened in 2011 in a former grain store in Oamaru’s heritage Victorian Precinct. It features a full-scale locomotive that shoots fire and smoke.

reviews.summary: Quirky, fun and surprisingly engaging. The fire-breathing train is a highlight.

pois.name: Dunedin Public Art Gallery
pois.short: NZ’s oldest public art gallery — strong in Impressionist works, Japanese prints and contemporary NZ art.
pois.commentary: Founded in 1884. Strengths include Japanese prints, European works, and an excellent NZ art collection. Free entry.

reviews.summary: An excellent regional gallery with collections that punch above its weight.

pois.name: Central Otago Wine Region
pois.short: The world’s southernmost wine region — producing Pinot Noir that rivals Burgundy.
pois.commentary: Central Otago is the world’s southernmost wine region. The continental climate produces concentrated, elegant Pinot Noir. Key cellar doors include Felton Road, Mt Difficulty, Amisfield.

reviews.summary: Wine lovers rate Central Otago’s Pinot Noir among the world’s best.

pois.name: Ōamaru Victorian Precinct
pois.short: New Zealand's most intact Victorian commercial streetscape — Ōamaru limestone facades, working studios, and the country's best penguin colony at dusk.
pois.commentary: The Ōamaru Historic Precinct runs along Harbour and Tyne Streets, about 500 m of continuous cream-white Ōamaru limestone commercial buildings dating from the 1870s–1890s. Unlike most New Zealand heritage main streets, almost no Victorian buildings have been demolished here — the town's post-1880 economic stagnation inadvertently preserved everything. Today the ground-floor tenancies are an unusually coherent cluster of independent studios, bookshops, galleries, and small-batch producers. The New Zealand Whisky Collection has its only outlet here; the precinct also holds the Ōamaru Opera House (1907), the oldest continuously operating opera house in the country. At dusk, the precinct is five minutes' walk from the Ōamaru Blue Penguin Colony — the world's smallest penguin coming ashore nightly.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently rate the Ōamaru precinct as one of the South Island's genuinely unique streetscapes — noting that it rewards a slow walk rather than a drive-through stop. The combination of heritage architecture, independent retail, and the evening penguin parade at Harbour Street makes it an easy full-afternoon itinerary. Reviewers recommend staying overnight to catch both the precinct and the penguin colony.

pois.name: Ōamaru Blue Penguin Colony
pois.short: The world's smallest penguin comes ashore nightly through the middle of town — a viewing stand, a colony of ~200, and a daytime visitors' area.
pois.commentary: Kororā — the little blue penguin — is the world's smallest penguin at 25–30 cm and around 1 kg. The Ōamaru colony is one of the most accessible in New Zealand: a managed site with a purpose-built viewing stand at the old railway wharf, where 150–200 penguins come ashore between dusk and full dark each evening after fishing at sea. The timing is advertised daily and is remarkably consistent. A daytime area allows viewing of nesting boxes during breeding season. Unlike many wildlife experiences, this one is commercial and managed — which means the birds are not disturbed, the access is reliable, and the interpretation is thorough. Combine with the Victorian Precinct immediately behind the site.

reviews.summary: Reviewers describe the Ōamaru penguin colony as one of the most reliably satisfying wildlife experiences in the South Island — the nightly parade is predictable, well-organised, and genuinely moving. The daytime area is praised as a bonus visit not everyone knows about. Most reviewers arrive for sunset and stay until the last penguin waddles past.

pois.name: Rangi Ruru Arts — Dunedin Street Art Quarter
pois.short: Dunedin's North End arts district — a concentrated cluster of large-scale murals, independent studios, and converted industrial spaces.
pois.commentary: Dunedin's North End — stretching from the Octagon north through Moray Place and up Cumberland and Stuart Streets — has developed since the mid-2010s as the city's informal arts and music quarter. The Creative Arts Precinct centred on the old radio station building and the Moray Gallery anchor. The area has a high density of large-scale commissioned murals, particularly on the lane walls off Stuart Street and around the Vogel and Jetty Street intersection — works by local and international artists including pieces tied to Dunedin's internationally ranked music scene (Flying Nun Records was born here in 1981). Not a formal trail, but a walkable quarter that rewards a 45-minute wander.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Dunedin's North End murals as a genuine and organic expression of the city's creative character, rather than a curated tourist product. Reviewers note it is best explored on foot with no fixed route — the best pieces are often the ones you stumble across in a lane. Pairs naturally with a coffee at a North End café.

pois.name: Carrington / Cardrona Alpine Resort — Snow Farm NZ
pois.short: New Zealand's only dedicated Nordic ski area — 55 km of groomed trails on the Pisa Range above Wānaka.
pois.commentary: Snow Farm NZ on the Pisa Range above Wānaka is the only dedicated cross-country and Nordic combined ski area in New Zealand, sitting at 1,550–1,700 m on the broad, wind-scoured plateau. Fifty-five kilometres of groomed trails wind through tussock snowfields with panoramic views of the Treble Cone massif and Lake Wānaka below. In summer the area becomes high-alpine farmland traversed by the Pisa Range loop walking tracks. The Snow Farm has hosted national and international cross-country and biathlon competitions.
