Access is via the same Cardrona Valley road as Cardrona Alpine Resort; the two sit 20 minutes apart. An entirely different experience from the downhill fields — quiet, open, and very Central Otago.

reviews.summary: Nordic skiers and snowshoers describe Snow Farm as one of the great secrets of the Wānaka winter — genuinely uncrowded even in peak season and dramatically scenic. Reviewers note it is completely unlike anything else in New Zealand's ski industry and rewards those looking for something beyond lift-accessed slopes. Summer hikers praise the wide-open plateau views.

pois.name: Central Stories Museum & Art Gallery, Alexandra
pois.short: Central Otago's regional museum — the story of gold, schist, and the Māori and settler cultures of the basin, told with care.
pois.commentary: Central Stories is the regional museum and art gallery for Central Otago, housed in a purpose-built space in Alexandra. The permanent collection covers the full arc of human occupation in the basin — Kāi Tahu use of the Clutha/Mata-Au pounamu routes, the Chinese and European gold rush from 1862, the agricultural and pastoral transformation, and the late 20th-century wine revolution. The exhibit on gold dredging is particularly strong — scale models, personal objects, and oral history recordings. The rotating art gallery shows regional artists working in the distinctive Central Otago palette of ochre, cobalt, and schist grey. Entry is a modest charge. Compact, high-quality, and typically uncrowded.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently describe Central Stories as punching well above its weight for a regional museum — the dredging exhibition and the Kāi Tahu pounamu story are called out as highlights. Reviewers note the art gallery programme is reliably worth a look and the staff are particularly knowledgeable. Best combined with a walk to the Alexandra clock hill above the town.

pois.name: Waitati Valley & Blueskin Bay
pois.short: A 30-minute drive north of Dunedin — an arts-and-music community in a tidal bay valley with an uncommonly good monthly market.
pois.commentary: The Waitati Valley above Blueskin Bay, 25 km north of Dunedin, has for decades attracted artists, writers, musicians, and alternative-lifers — a concentration unusual in rural Otago. The community is genuinely self-organised: the Blueskin Resilient Communities Trust, a community-owned wind project, and the Waitati Music Festival (held annually since 1996) are all expressions of this. The monthly Blueskin Bay market at the Waitati Hall draws regional producers and craft makers. The tidal bay itself is a birding spot — royal spoonbill, white heron, and variable oystercatcher are regular. Drive up the valley from Waitati township into the rural backblocks for the full texture.

reviews.summary: Visitors who discover Waitati consistently describe it as an authentic counter to Dunedin's tourism circuit — genuinely inhabited, uncommercial, and with a warmth of community that is hard to manufacture. The monthly market is praised by locals and visitors alike. Best on a fine Saturday morning.

pois.name: Ōtākou Marae & Kāi Tahu Headstone Cemetery
pois.short: The ancestral Ngāi Tahu settlement at the entrance to Otago Harbour — source of the region's name and site of the 1840 Treaty signing.
pois.commentary: Ōtākou is the settlement whose name, anglicised, became 'Otago'. Located at the outer harbour entrance on the Otago Peninsula, it was a major Kāi Tahu kāinga and from the 1820s the site of some of New Zealand's most intensive shore-based whaling. On 13 June 1840 the rangatira of Kāi Tahu at Ōtākou signed Te Tiriti o Waitangi aboard HMS *Herald* — one of the most significant southern signings. The marae complex is not open to casual visitors, but the adjacent cemetery contains graves of Ngāi Tahu leaders including Karetai (Māori Land Court judge) and of early British and American whalers — an unusual co-burial that reflects the genuine interconnection of these communities. Interpretation panels on the road verge above the shore provide respectful context.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Ōtākou as quietly powerful — a place where the Otago region's deepest history is physically present. Reviewers appreciate that the site is not commercially developed and urge respectful behaviour near the marae boundary. The headstone cemetery is consistently called the most moving stop on the Peninsula drive.

pois.name: Te Rūnanga o Moeraki — Ōnekakara / Moeraki Marae
pois.short: The Kāi Tahu rūnanga of Moeraki — a coastal kāinga where the legend of the Ōnawe pounamu and the Āraiteuru canoe is held.
pois.commentary: Moeraki — the village on the north Otago coast near the famous boulders — is a Kāi Tahu settlement whose continuous occupation predates European contact by centuries. The rūnanga of Te Rūnanga o Moeraki is one of the eight Kāi Tahu papatipu rūnanga. The boulders that tourists photograph from the beach are kaihinaki in Kāi Tahu tradition — the food baskets washed ashore from the great canoe *Āraiteuru* when it foundered here. The marae is not a tourist site, but Fleur's Place restaurant in the village (one of New Zealand's most-praised seafood restaurants, run from a converted fishing shed) is owned and operated by the community and serves as a legitimate cultural connection point. Respectful roadside viewing of the marae structures is possible.

reviews.summary: Visitors note that understanding the Kāi Tahu meaning of the Moeraki Boulders transforms what might be a geological curiosity into a genuinely moving site. Fleur's Place is called one of the best and most authentic seafood experiences in New Zealand by reviewers who seek it out. The combination of cultural site and exceptional restaurant makes Moeraki a genuine half-day stop.

pois.name: Hakataramea Valley & Pass
pois.short: A remote valley and historic pass used for centuries by Kāi Tahu as a pounamu route between Canterbury and Otago.
pois.commentary: The Hakataramea Valley cuts south from the Mackenzie Basin into the North Otago hinterland and over a low pass into the Waitaki drainage. For Kāi Tahu, this route was a recognised ara tawhito — an ancient travel road — used to carry pounamu (greenstone) from the West Coast sources north to Canterbury and south to coastal settlements. The valley itself is broad sheep-country farmland today, largely unchanged in land use since the 1850s. State Highway 83 passes through the lower valley, and a backcountry road continues to the pass. The historical signage at the Hakataramea junction on SH83 is minimal — this is a landscape that rewards the traveller who knows its deeper history. DOC's *Te Ara Pounamu* documentation covers this corridor.

reviews.summary: Visitors who know the Kāi Tahu pounamu route history find the valley deeply evocative — the unmodified landscape makes it easy to picture the generations of travel. Reviewers note it is not a signposted attraction and is best appreciated by those who have read some background before arriving. A genuinely uncommerced stop on the road between Christchurch and Central Otago.

pois.name: Tūhuru / Māori Rock Art, Duntroon Limestone Cliffs
pois.short: Kāi Tahu charcoal drawings on a limestone overhang — some of the best-preserved Māori rock art on the South Island.
pois.commentary: The Duntroon area of the upper Waitaki Valley contains the densest concentration of Māori rock art in New Zealand — drawings made in charcoal and red ochre on limestone overhangs, sheltered from rain, by Kāi Tahu and their predecessors over several hundred years. The Ngā Tāngata Tiaki o Waitaha (the Waitaha guardians) are the primary kaitiaki of these sites. The most accessible sites are at Takiroa, just north of Duntroon on SH83 — a marked DOC site with interpretation panels covering both the images and the cultural protocols. Images include animals (moa, lizard-like figures, dogs), geometric forms, and human figures. Photography is discouraged at many sites; the DOC panels explain which rules apply.

reviews.summary: Reviewers describe the Duntroon rock art as a revelation — among the most significant but least-publicised Māori cultural sites in the South Island. Visitors who follow the DOC protocols describe the experience as deeply respectful and absorbing. Most reviewers note they could easily have driven past without knowing these shelters existed.

pois.name: Kāi Tahu Cultural Landscape — Maniototo / Te Koroka
pois.short: The wide Maniototo basin — Te Koroka in Māori — one of the great moa-hunting landscapes of pre-European Aotearoa.
pois.commentary: The Maniototo basin — Te Koroka, meaning 'the long red one' for its ochre tussock in autumn — was a significant seasonal resource area for Kāi Tahu. Archaeological evidence documents moa-hunting camps across the basin floor from at least the 14th century; many middens and camp sites are protected but not publicised to prevent looting. The basin's flatness and relatively mild winters (by Central Otago standards) made it a movement corridor for parties travelling between Dunedin coastal settlements and inland pounamu routes. Ranfurly is the service town; the Maniototo Scarecrow Festival and the Otago Central Rail Trail pass through. The Maniototo Early Settlers Museum at Naseby holds some local archaeological interpretation.

reviews.summary: Visitors who approach the Maniototo with an understanding of its Kāi Tahu history describe it as one of the most historically layered landscapes in New Zealand — the flat basin and the schist outcrops become something else entirely when the moa-hunting context is understood. Reviewers note the cultural story is not well told on the ground and reward independent reading before arrival.

pois.name: Fleur's Place, Moeraki
pois.short: New Zealand's most celebrated quirky seafood restaurant — a converted fishing shed on the Moeraki wharf, serving the catch of the day since 2002.
pois.commentary: Fleur Sullivan opened Fleur's Place in 2002 in a converted fishing shed directly on the Moeraki boat ramp — no sea view in any conventional sense, just the smell of salt, the sound of rigging, and a blackboard menu that changes entirely with what came off the boats that morning. Blue cod, flounder, crayfish, and whitebait are the regulars. The wine list is serious Central Otago and Marlborough. The building is deliberately rough: timber boards, mismatched chairs, a coal range. It has been written about in *The Guardian*, *Saveur*, and virtually every serious New Zealand food guide. Reservations are essential and fill weeks in advance. Drive north from Dunedin on SH1, turn off at Moeraki — the detour adds 15 minutes and is non-negotiable.

reviews.summary: Reviewers consistently call Fleur's Place one of the best meals in New Zealand — not for formal technique but for the absolute freshness of the fish and the irreproducible atmosphere. The blue cod and the flounder are called out in almost every review. Reservations are described as essential — walk-ins are near-impossible in summer.

pois.name: Carrick Winery, Bannockburn
pois.short: A Bannockburn estate cellar door in the hottest, driest sub-region of Central Otago — terrace wines above the Kawarau gorge.
pois.commentary: Bannockburn is the sub-region that many Central Otago winemakers regard as the finest in the district — a north-facing schist terrace above the Kawarau River gorge, with the highest summer temperatures in New Zealand wine country. Carrick is one of Bannockburn's benchmark estates, certified organic, with a well-regarded cellar door and bistro. The pinot noir and chardonnay are consistently awarded at national level. The setting — schist rock walls, lavender borders, views down the gorge — is the quintessential Central Otago wine experience. Other notable Bannockburn cellars in the same immediate area: Felton Road (widely regarded as NZ's greatest pinot producer; cellar door by appointment) and Olssens.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Carrick as the complete Central Otago cellar door experience — exceptional wine, beautiful setting, and a bistro menu that uses the same seasonal produce as the estate. Reviewers consistently recommend allowing at least 90 minutes. The organic certification and the Bannockburn terrace setting are frequently highlighted.

pois.name: Vogel Street Kitchen & Precinct, Dunedin
pois.short: Dunedin's converted warehouse food precinct — the city's most creative cluster of cafés, wine bars, and artisan producers.
pois.commentary: The Vogel Street Kitchen and the surrounding Warehouse Precinct south of the railway station represent Dunedin's most deliberate food-and-drink cluster — a series of mid-19th-century merchant warehouses converted into restaurants, roasters, breweries, and galleries. The anchor is Vogel Street Kitchen itself, known for long shared tables and a seasonal menu that leans on South Island produce. Within 200 metres: Emerson's Brewery taproom (Dunedin's founding craft brewery, now owned by Lion but still brewing on site), Fix & Fogg, Strictly Coffee. The precinct is most alive on Friday evenings and Saturday mornings. Part of Dunedin's active post-earthquake re-imagining of its southern edge.

reviews.summary: Reviewers describe the Vogel Street precinct as the best representation of modern Dunedin's food culture — warehouse aesthetics, excellent coffee, serious wine, and a kitchen menu that avoids tourist clichés. Emerson's taproom is called a compulsory stop by beer reviewers. Most visitors recommend a Friday-evening visit when the precinct is at full energy.

pois.name: Ranfurly Art Deco Town
pois.short: New Zealand's least-visited Art Deco town — Ranfurly's 1930s main street, rebuilt after fire, with a striking cluster of Streamline Moderne buildings.
pois.commentary: Ranfurly, the main town of the Maniototo basin on the Otago Central Rail Trail, has an Art Deco main street that is almost entirely unknown outside the cycling community. The town was substantially rebuilt after a 1930s fire, at the same moment New Zealand's civic architecture embraced Streamline Moderne. The result is a compact block of cream and pastel rendered facades — the Ranfurly Hotel, the old ANZ Bank, the former cinema — that looks more like provincial rural America than the South Island. The Maniototo town itself (population ~900) has a curious, dry-air intensity. The Rail Trail passes through, and the town is the logical mid-point stop for Clyde-to-Middlemarch riders.

reviews.summary: Rail Trail cyclists and road-trippers who know to look for Ranfurly's architecture describe it as a genuine surprise — a coherent Art Deco streetscape with none of the Napier crowds. Reviewers note the Ranfurly Hotel still serves meals and is worth stopping for. The town's scale (walkable in 20 minutes) makes it an easy half-hour stop on any itinerary.

pois.name: Taiaroa Head Royal Albatross Centre
pois.short: The world's only mainland royal albatross colony, where northern royal albatrosses nest and raise chicks within sight of Dunedin at the tip of the Otago Peninsula.
pois.commentary: Taiaroa Head is home to the world's only mainland breeding colony of northern royal albatrosses, a population that established itself on this exposed headland in 1919 and now numbers over 100 nesting pairs. Guided tours from the Royal Albatross Centre allow visitors to observe the colony from purpose-built hides, watching the enormous birds — with wingspans of up to 3 metres — glide on the constant sea winds directly overhead. The centre also provides access to Fort Taiaroa, a 19th-century coastal defence installation with an extraordinary Armstrong Disappearing Gun, one of only two surviving examples in the world. The headland is also shared by a colony of New Zealand fur seals visible from the clifftops.

reviews.summary: The Taiaroa Head albatross experience is rated as one of New Zealand's most extraordinary wildlife encounters, with the sheer scale of the birds in flight at close range being genuinely breathtaking. Pre-booking is essential.

pois.name: Kawarau Bridge Bungy Jump
pois.short: The world's first commercial bungee jump, operating since 1988 from the historic Kawarau Gorge Suspension Bridge 43 metres above the turquoise river.
pois.commentary: The Kawarau Bridge is where AJ Hackett and Henry van Asch launched the world's commercial bungee jumping industry in November 1988, and the 1880-built suspension bridge over the Kawarau River gorge remains the most iconic and historically significant bungee venue on earth. Jumpers leap 43 metres from the bridge towards the brilliant turquoise river below, with the option of requesting a water touch as part of the experience. The Kawarau Bungy Centre on the gorge rim above the bridge includes a museum tracing the history of bungee jumping, viewing platforms for spectators, and a zipride across the gorge. The gorge itself is dramatic, carved through schist rock within the Gibbston wine country 23 kilometres east of Queenstown.

reviews.summary: The historical significance and the dramatic gorge setting make Kawarau Bridge the most respected of Queenstown's bungee sites. Spectators on the viewing platforms report that watching others jump is almost as exciting as jumping themselves.

pois.name: Nevis Bungy
pois.short: New Zealand's highest bungee jump at 134 metres from a suspended pod in the remote Nevis Valley, delivering 8.5 seconds of free fall above a dramatic alpine gorge.
pois.commentary: The Nevis Bungy is New Zealand's highest bungee jump and one of the highest in the world, suspended from a pod hanging from cables stretched across the remote Nevis Gorge in the mountains above Queenstown. The platform is accessible only via a 45-minute 4WD journey from Queenstown followed by a cable car ride to the pod suspended above the valley. The 134-metre drop delivers 8.5 seconds of free fall before the cord engages, during which the entire Nevis Valley landscape — tussock hills, schist bluffs, and alpine sky — rotates through the field of vision. The experience is operated by AJ Hackett Bungy, who also run Kawarau, but the Nevis is exclusively aimed at those seeking the maximum level of commitment.

reviews.summary: Described by jumpers as categorically different from lower bungee experiences, with the extended free fall, remote alpine setting, and sheer height creating an intensity that many describe as life-altering. The remote valley approach builds anticipation effectively.

pois.name: Wanaka Deep Canyon Canyoning
pois.short: New Zealand's premier canyoning experience in the alpine pools and polished chutes of the Matukituki River beneath Mount Aspiring National Park.
pois.commentary: Deep Canyon has been guiding canyoning descents in the rivers and gorges around Wanaka since 1994, the first company in New Zealand to do so, and operates in the extraordinary landscape of Mount Aspiring National Park and the Matukituki River catchment. Participants jump, slide, swim, and abseil through deep narrow slot canyons scoured from schist rock by glacial meltwater, with crystal-clear pools at the base of each drop. The Leaping Burn Canyon is Deep Canyon's signature experience, a full-day adventure involving 30-metre abseils, natural waterslides, cliff jumps of up to 10 metres, and long swims through sculpted gorges. The surrounding mountain scenery — glaciated peaks, hanging valleys, and beech forest — elevates the experience beyond the canyon itself.

reviews.summary: Consistently rated among the finest adventure experiences in the South Island, with the combination of the natural canyon beauty, the variety of challenges, and the Mount Aspiring scenery making it exceptional. Experienced guides are praised for safety management and commentary.

pois.name: Routeburn Canyon Canyoning
pois.short: A full-day canyoning adventure through the Routeburn Valley, a World Heritage mountain landscape used as filming locations for The Lord of the Rings trilogy.
pois.commentary: The Routeburn Valley near Queenstown lies within the Te Wahipounamu World Heritage Area and the canyon descents here combine world-class adventure with one of New Zealand's most celebrated mountain landscapes. The full-day trip with Canyon Explorers involves zip-lining, abseiling, jumping, and swimming through the Routeburn Canyon, with the dramatic valley walls and beech forest backdrop providing constant visual drama. The valley is widely recognised as a Lord of the Rings filming location — the mountainscape appears repeatedly in both the original trilogy and The Hobbit films. The canyon sections themselves are demanding and wet, requiring a full wetsuit, and the remote location accessed by vehicle and foot from the Routeburn Track carpark adds to the sense of genuine wilderness adventure.

reviews.summary: The Routeburn Canyon combines technical canyoning challenges with one of New Zealand's most iconic mountain settings, creating an adventure that satisfies both thrill-seekers and landscape enthusiasts. The Lord of the Rings context resonates strongly with international visitors.

pois.name: Otago Peninsula Yellow-Eyed Penguin Reserve
pois.short: A privately operated penguin reserve on the Otago Peninsula with underground hides allowing observation of nesting yellow-eyed penguins on their home beach.
pois.commentary: The Penguin Place reserve at Harington Point on the Otago Peninsula is a pioneering conservation initiative that began in 1985 when the McGrouther family began protecting yellow-eyed penguin nesting habitat on their farmland. The reserve operates guided tours through an extensive network of camouflaged tunnels and underground hides dug into the dunes, allowing visitors to observe penguins at nest sites without causing the stress that open exposure to humans creates. Yellow-eyed penguins — the world's rarest penguin species — are monitored and managed here with an active hospital rehabilitation programme for injured birds. The reserve approach involves genuine conservation commitment, with tour revenue directly funding habitat restoration and veterinary care.

reviews.summary: Penguin Place is consistently praised for the quality of its conservation operation and the extraordinary closeness of observation achieved through the hide system. The underground tunnels and the rehabilitation hospital add depth beyond simple wildlife viewing.

pois.name: Nugget Point Lighthouse and Wildlife Reserve
pois.short: An iconic lighthouse headland in the northern Catlins with dramatic sea stacks below supporting New Zealand sea lions, fur seals, and nesting spotted shags.
pois.commentary: Nugget Point is the most striking coastal landform in the Catlins, a narrow rocky promontory crowned by a lighthouse built in 1869 and flanked by a series of dramatic sea stacks — the 'nuggets' — that break the surface of the Southern Ocean below. The rocks and surrounding waters support one of the South Island's most accessible concentrations of wildlife, with New Zealand sea lions (the world's rarest sea lion species), New Zealand fur seals, yellow-eyed penguins, and Foveaux shags all present year-round. The 15-minute walk from the carpark to the lighthouse passes interpretive panels about the geology and wildlife, and the clifftop viewpoint from the lighthouse provides the full 180-degree panorama of the sea stack formations below. Evening visits during summer allow penguin watching as birds return from sea.

reviews.summary: Nugget Point is consistently rated as the most rewarding single wildlife stop in the Catlins, combining dramatic coastal geology with accessible multi-species wildlife viewing at no cost. The lighthouse itself and the stack formations are also photographically exceptional.

pois.name: Steampunk HQ / Oamaru
pois.short: Immersive steampunk art gallery and sculpture park in a converted grain elevator.
pois.commentary: Steampunk HQ occupies a converted Victorian-era grain elevator in Oamaru's historic precinct, housing kinetic sculptures, interactive machines, and dystopian art installations that celebrate the steampunk aesthetic. The exterior features a full-size steam locomotive erupting from the building. Oamaru has embraced the steampunk identity wholeheartedly, with an annual Steampunk Festival.

reviews.summary: Delightfully weird and wonderful. The sculptures are genuinely impressive. Perfect complement to Oamaru's Victorian precinct.

pois.name: Otago Central Rail Trail
pois.short: 152km heritage rail trail cycling through Central Otago goldfields and tussock country.
pois.commentary: The Otago Central Rail Trail was New Zealand's first cycle trail, following the route of the disused Otago Central Railway for 152 kilometres between Middlemarch and Clyde. The trail passes through historic gold-mining towns, crosses towering viaducts, and traverses vast tussock-covered hills. Most riders take 3-4 days. The trail is flat to gently graded making it accessible to moderate cyclists.

reviews.summary: New Zealand's classic cycle trail. The scenery changes constantly and the small-town hospitality is wonderful.

pois.name: Blue Pools Walk / Makarora
pois.short: Short bush walk to crystal-clear blue pools on the Makarora River.
pois.commentary: The Blue Pools Walk is a 30-minute return track through silver beech forest to two stunning blue pools on the Makarora River, connected by a swing bridge that provides the classic viewpoint. The pools get their vivid blue colour from the glacial origins of the water and the smooth schist riverbed. Located on SH6 between Wanaka and Haast Pass, it's a perfect 30-minute leg-stretch on a long drive.

reviews.summary: The water colour is unreal. The swing bridge view is the classic shot. Quick and easy stop on the Haast Pass road.

pois.name: Cardrona Hotel
pois.short: Iconic 1863 gold-rush pub between Wanaka and Queenstown, one of NZ's most photographed buildings.
pois.commentary: The Cardrona Hotel is one of New Zealand's most photographed heritage buildings — a corrugated iron pub standing alone in the tussock-covered Crown Range landscape between Wanaka and Queenstown. Built in 1863 during the Otago gold rush, the hotel has operated almost continuously for over 160 years. The rustic bar is decorated with memorabilia spanning the pub's history. The hotel sits on the Crown Range Road, New Zealand's highest sealed road.

reviews.summary: Dripping with character and history. The setting is spectacular. Stop for a beer and a photo.

pois.name: Ranfurly Art Deco Town
pois.short: Small Maniototo town with collection of rural Art Deco buildings.
pois.commentary: Ranfurly, the main town of the Maniototo plain in Central Otago, features an unexpected collection of Art Deco buildings dating from the 1930s, earning it the title of Art Deco Capital of Rural New Zealand. Key buildings include the Centennial Milk Bar with its original soda fountain, the Ranfurly Hotel, and several commercial buildings along the main street. A self-guided heritage walk covers the main buildings. The town sits on the Otago Central Rail Trail, making it a popular overnight stop for cyclists. The Maniototo's vast, dry, golden landscape under enormous skies has an almost American West quality. The town's Friday evening gatherings at the historic Railway Hotel are legendary among rail trail riders.

reviews.summary: Charming Art Deco surprise in deep rural NZ. The Centennial Milk Bar is a time capsule. Great rail trail stopover.

pois.name: Alexandra & Clyde Heritage Precinct
pois.short: Twin gold-rush towns with stone heritage buildings and Central Otago fruit orchards.
pois.commentary: Alexandra and neighbouring Clyde are Central Otago towns with rich gold-rush heritage visible in their stone buildings, miners' cottages, and the massive Clyde Dam on the Clutha River. Clyde's main street is particularly well-preserved, with stone buildings from the 1860s housing excellent cafes, galleries, and boutiques. Alexandra is known for its extreme climate — the hottest summers and coldest winters in New Zealand — and the fruit orchards that thrive in the dry heat, producing apricots, cherries, and stone fruit. The Alexandra Blossom Festival in September celebrates the spring bloom. The Otago Central Rail Trail begins (or ends) at Clyde, and the Clutha Gold Trail cycle path connects both towns. The Central Stories Museum covers the region's Maori, gold, and agricultural history.

reviews.summary: Clyde's stone precinct is beautifully preserved. Summer fruit from roadside stalls is exceptional. Excellent cycling connections.

pois.name: Port Chalmers / Artist Quarter
pois.short: Historic port town with vibrant artist community and heritage architecture.
pois.commentary: Port Chalmers, Dunedin's harbour suburb, has evolved from a working port into one of New Zealand's most vibrant small artist communities. Galleries, studios, and creative businesses occupy heritage stone buildings along the main street, with artists drawn by affordable rents, dramatic harbour views, and an independent spirit. The town was the departure point for Antarctic expeditions by Scott and Shackleton, commemorated at the Port Chalmers Seafarers Museum. The Flagstaff Gallery and Iona Church are worth visiting. Regular arts events and open studio weekends draw visitors from across Otago. The Otago Harbour views from the lookout above town are spectacular, and the ferry connection to Dunedin adds convenience.

reviews.summary: A genuinely creative community with excellent galleries. The Antarctic history connection is fascinating. Beautiful harbour setting.

pois.name: Baldwin Street — World's Steepest Street
pois.short: Officially the world's steepest residential street — a gradient of 34.8% in suburban Dunedin.
pois.commentary: Baldwin Street in suburban Dunedin holds the Guinness World Record as the world's steepest residential street, with a maximum gradient of 34.8% (or 1 in 2.86). The street rises 69.2 metres over its 350-metre length, with the steepest section near the top surfaced in concrete rather than asphalt (which would melt and slide downhill in summer). Walking to the top is a genuine cardiovascular challenge — the gradient is punishing but the view from the top and the bragging rights make it worthwhile. The annual Baldwin Street Gutbuster charity race draws runners (and many walkers) who tackle the hill. It's a quintessentially quirky Dunedin attraction.

reviews.summary: Steeper than you think — and the walk down is harder than the walk up. Quick visit but very satisfying. Good city views from the top.

pois.name: The Lost Gypsy Gallery
pois.short: Eccentric automata museum in a bus and gallery in the Catlins.
pois.commentary: The Lost Gypsy Gallery is one of New Zealand's most eccentric and delightful attractions — a collection of handmade automata, mechanical curiosities, and kinetic art housed in a repurposed bus and gallery in the tiny Catlins settlement of Papatowai. Created by Blair Somerville over many years, the gallery encourages visitors to push every button, wind every handle, and peer into every peephole to discover intricate mechanical contraptions. Inside the bus, dozens of automata perform surprising and humorous actions. The gallery building features larger installations and sculptures. It's a celebration of creativity, ingenuity, and the joy of mechanical motion. Located in the remote Catlins, it's a highlight of any Southern Scenic Route road trip.

reviews.summary: Visitors of all ages are charmed and amazed. The automata are ingenious and often hilarious. Many describe it as the highlight of their Catlins trip. Don't miss the bus.

pois.name: Ōrokonui Ecosanctuary Guided Tour
pois.short: A predator-fenced ecosanctuary near Dunedin sheltering rare native birds including kākā, takahē and kiwi.
pois.commentary: Ōrokonui Ecosanctuary protects 307 hectares of coastal forest north of Dunedin behind a predator-proof fence. Inside, the forest is alive with species that have disappeared from most of mainland New Zealand — South Island kākā, takahē, kiwi, jewelled gecko and tuatara. Guided tours run daily and dramatically increase your chances of seeing the rarer residents. The guides are passionate conservationists who bring the ecology to life. Self-guided walks are also available on well-maintained tracks through the regenerating bush. It's a working model of what New Zealand's forests could sound like everywhere.

reviews.summary: Guided tours are excellent for spotting rare species. The forest is beautiful and the birdsong remarkable. Some note the walk is hilly. A moving example of conservation in action.

pois.name: Tunnel Beach Coastal Walk
pois.short: A hand-hewn tunnel cut through sandstone cliffs leads to a dramatic hidden beach south of Dunedin.
pois.commentary: Tunnel Beach is reached by a 20-minute walk from the car park, descending through farmland to dramatic sandstone cliffs. The highlight is a hand-carved tunnel cut through the headland in the 1870s by politician John Cargill so his family could access the beach below. The tunnel opens onto a wild stretch of coast with towering sea stacks, natural arches and pounding surf. The geological formations — carved by millennia of wave action — are extraordinary. Swimming is dangerous but the landscape photography is world-class. The track is closed during lambing season (August-October).

reviews.summary: The tunnel and sea stacks are breathtaking. The walk is steep but short. Closed during lambing — check DOC website. Photography opportunities are exceptional. Not suitable for swimming.

pois.name: Armstrong Disappearing Gun, Taiaroa Head
pois.short: One of only two Armstrong Disappearing Guns remaining in the world — a Victorian-era marvel of military engineering at Taiaroa Head.
pois.commentary: At the tip of the Otago Peninsula, alongside the famous albatross colony, sits one of the rarest pieces of military hardware on earth. The Armstrong Disappearing Gun was installed in 1886 as part of Dunedin's Russian Scare coastal defences. The ingenious mechanism used the gun's recoil to lower it behind a parapet for reloading, making it nearly invisible to enemy ships. Only one other example survives worldwide. Guided tours explain the engineering and the geopolitical paranoia that put a state-of-the-art weapon on a headland in the South Pacific. It's the kind of obscure historical footnote that New Zealand does brilliantly.

reviews.summary: A fascinating piece of military history. Guided tours bring the engineering to life. Easily combined with the albatross colony visit. Some note the tours are weather-dependent.

pois.name: Emerson's Brewery & Taproom, Dunedin
pois.short: The godfather of New Zealand craft beer — Emerson's taproom in Dunedin pours 20+ beers with excellent food to match.
pois.commentary: Richard Emerson started brewing in Dunedin in 1992, well before craft beer was fashionable, and his Pilsner and Bookbinder remain benchmarks of New Zealand brewing. The purpose-built taproom and restaurant in Anzac Avenue has over 20 beers on tap including seasonal specials you won't find anywhere else, paired with a menu designed to complement them. Brewery tours run regularly and offer insight into both the brewing process and Emerson's role in pioneering the New Zealand craft beer movement. Even if you're not a beer enthusiast, the Bookbinder session ale and a plate of beer-battered fish will convert you.

reviews.summary: Outstanding beer and food in an impressive space. The brewery tour is informative and fun. The Pilsner and Bookbinder are must-tries. Can get busy at weekends — book ahead.

pois.name: Otago Harbour Heritage Cruise
pois.short: A harbour cruise from Dunedin along the Otago Peninsula — albatross, seals, penguins and colonial history from the water.
pois.commentary: Cruising Otago Harbour by boat offers a completely different perspective on the peninsula's wildlife and heritage. Departing from central Dunedin, the cruise follows the harbour to Taiaroa Head, passing Port Chalmers, Quarantine Island, and the dramatic volcanic cliffs of the peninsula. Wildlife encounters may include albatross, blue penguins, fur seals and shags, while the guide narrates the harbour's Māori and colonial history. The return journey at dusk is particularly special as blue penguins begin arriving at their nesting sites along the harbour edge.

reviews.summary: A relaxing way to see the peninsula wildlife. The guides are knowledgeable about both ecology and history. Afternoon departures offer the best penguin viewing. Can be cold — dress warmly.

pois.name: Oamaru Victorian Heritage Walking Tour
pois.short: Walk through New Zealand's most complete Victorian streetscape — grand whitestone buildings, steampunk art, and a thriving creative community.
pois.commentary: Oamaru's Victorian Precinct is New Zealand's most architecturally complete 19th-century streetscape, built from local Oamaru whitestone during the 1880s boom years. The grand warehouses and commercial buildings now house galleries, cafés, vintage shops and studios, including the famous Steampunk HQ with its industrial-age art installations. A self-guided heritage walk traces the rise and fall and renaissance of the precinct through interpretive panels and QR codes. The Harbour-Tyne area comes alive on Heritage Sunday events with period costumes, penny-farthing rides and live music. The contrast between the grand architecture and the creative community that inhabits it makes Oamaru unique.

reviews.summary: A beautifully preserved Victorian streetscape. The creative community adds life. Steampunk HQ is a highlight. The whitestone architecture is stunning. Allow time to explore the galleries and shops.

pois.name: Macraes Flat — Grand & Otago Skink Conservation
pois.short: The last stronghold of two of New Zealand's rarest reptiles — the grand skink and Otago skink — in dramatic tussock-covered schist country.
pois.commentary: Macraes Flat in eastern Otago is the primary remaining habitat for the critically endangered grand skink and Otago skink — two large, striking reptiles found nowhere else on earth. DOC's conservation programme here includes predator control, habitat protection and a public viewing enclosure where you can reliably see both species basking on schist rock outcrops. The surrounding landscape of tussock-covered hills and exposed schist tor formations is hauntingly beautiful in itself. The skinks are most active on warm, sunny days — arrive mid-morning for best viewing. A small car park and interpretation panel mark the viewing site.

reviews.summary: A unique wildlife experience for reptile enthusiasts. The viewing enclosure is well-designed. Best on warm sunny days when skinks are basking. The landscape is dramatic. Worth the detour from SH85.

pois.name: Otago Central Rail Trail — Gold Towns to Vineyards
pois.short: New Zealand's original Great Ride — 152 km of car-free cycling through Central Otago's gold-town heritage and dramatic tussock landscapes.
pois.commentary: The Otago Central Rail Trail was New Zealand's first Great Ride and remains one of its best — 152 km of gentle, off-road cycling along the former Otago Central Railway between Middlemarch and Clyde. The trail passes through some of the most dramatic and sparsely populated landscape in the country: tussock-covered ranges, schist gorges, gold-rush towns, and the sun-drenched vineyards of Bannockburn. Most riders take 3-4 days, staying in characterful country pubs and B&Bs along the way. The trail is flat to gently graded (it was a railway) and suitable for most fitness levels. E-bike hire makes it accessible to all.

reviews.summary: One of New Zealand's great cycling experiences. The landscape is stunning and the heritage fascinating. Country pubs along the way are excellent. E-bikes make it accessible to all. Book accommodation ahead in summer.

pois.name: Deep Canyon — Wanaka Canyoning
pois.short: Abseil waterfalls, slide down natural chutes and leap into crystal-clear pools — world-class canyoning in the Wanaka backcountry.
pois.commentary: Wanaka offers some of the best canyoning in New Zealand, with operators like Deep Canyon taking groups through spectacular alpine canyons involving abseiling down waterfalls, sliding down natural rock chutes, jumping into deep pools and navigating through narrow rock channels. No prior experience is needed — guides provide all equipment and instruction. The canyon environments are stunning: polished schist walls, crystal-clear pools, cascading waterfalls and pockets of native bush. Multiple trip options range from half-day introductions to full-day expeditions in remote backcountry. It's one of the most immersive ways to experience the South Island's landscape.

reviews.summary: An exhilarating adventure in stunning scenery. Guides are professional and encouraging. Suitable for beginners. The water is cold but wetsuits keep you warm. One of the best activities in the Wanaka area.

pois.name: Dunedin Brewery & Distillery Trail
pois.short: Self-guided trail through Dunedin's 10 breweries and distilleries.
pois.commentary: Dunedin has over 150 years of brewing history with 10 breweries and distilleries producing 80+ beers and spirits. The trail spans from historic Speight's (NZ's oldest brewery, built on a natural spring) to boutique craft operators like Saddle Hill and Emerson's.

reviews.summary: Excellent beer trail — Speight's heritage tour is a highlight.

pois.name: Naseby Heritage Village & Ice Rink
pois.short: Gold rush heritage village with New Zealand's only outdoor curling rink.
pois.commentary: Naseby is a tiny gold rush village that time forgot, sitting at 600 metres in the Maniototo. Its historic buildings, ancient forest, and claim to fame as New Zealand's curling capital make it a unique diversion. The natural ice rink operates in winter, and the heritage trail tells the gold story.

reviews.summary: Charming heritage village — curling on natural ice is a blast.

pois.name: Hayes Engineering Works — Oturehua
pois.short: Preserved Victorian engineering workshop of a self-taught rural inventor.
pois.commentary: Ernest Hayes was a self-taught engineer who built an ingenious workshop in remote Oturehua, powered by a water wheel. His inventions — including a famous wire strainer used across NZ farms — are preserved in situ with original belt-driven machinery. A time capsule of Kiwi ingenuity.

reviews.summary: Incredible Kiwi ingenuity — a fascinating heritage gem.

pois.name: Evansdale Cheese
pois.short: Artisan cheesemaker near Dunedin producing award-winning farmhouse cheeses.
pois.commentary: Evansdale Cheese has been handcrafting cheese in small batches since 1978, making it one of New Zealand's pioneering artisan cheesemakers. Their factory shop offers tastings of their full range including the famous Puhoi-style brie and vintage cheddar.

reviews.summary: Excellent artisan cheese — the factory shop is worth a detour.

pois.name: Moeraki Boulders Coastal Walk
pois.short: Coastal walk past mysterious spherical boulders on Koekohe Beach.
pois.commentary: The Moeraki Boulders are large spherical concretions formed over millions of years on the ocean floor, now exposed on the beach by erosion. The coastal walk connects the boulders with Moeraki village and its excellent seafood restaurant — geology meets gastronomy.

reviews.summary: Fascinating geological formations — best visited at low tide.

pois.name: Ophir Historic Village
pois.short: Tiny gold-rush village preserved in stone on the Otago Central Rail Trail.
pois.commentary: Ophir is a perfectly preserved gold-rush village where stone buildings, including the 1886 post office and Daniel O'Connell Bridge, remain virtually unchanged. The village sits on the Otago Central Rail Trail and holds New Zealand's record for the coldest temperature ever recorded (-21.6°C).

reviews.summary: Beautiful stone buildings frozen in time — a true heritage gem.

pois.name: Katiki Point — Yellow-eyed Penguin Viewing
pois.short: Free viewing of endangered yellow-eyed penguins near Moeraki.
pois.commentary: Katiki Point lighthouse area provides free viewing of hoiho (yellow-eyed penguins), one of the world's rarest penguin species. A short walk from the lighthouse leads to hides where penguins can be observed returning from the sea in the late afternoon — no booking required.

reviews.summary: Amazing to see these rare penguins for free — be patient and respectful.

pois.name: Blue Lake, St Bathans
pois.short: A strange blue mining lake below pale clay cliffs beside one of Central Otago's most atmospheric old towns.
pois.commentary: Blue Lake at St Bathans looks natural only until you learn how violently it was made. Hydraulic gold sluicing carved away Kildare Hill, leaving pale clay cliffs and a deep pit that filled with mineral-tinted water. The lake now sits beside the tiny historic settlement of St Bathans, where the Vulcan Hotel and old streets give the place its half-remembered goldfield atmosphere. It is photogenic, but the best visit is slower than a snapshot: walk the lake edge, look at the carved ground, then read the town as a survival of boom, collapse and reinvention.

reviews.summary: Popular for photography, mining history and the old-town atmosphere; visitors caution against getting too close to unstable edges.

pois.name: Sutton Salt Lake
pois.short: New Zealand’s only inland salt lake, reached by a dryland loop walk in the Strath Taieri.
pois.commentary: Sutton Salt Lake feels almost misplaced in inland Otago: a pale saline basin held in dry tussock country. The loop walk is easy, but the landscape is unusual enough to make you slow down and notice salt, wind and geology doing quiet work.

pois.name: Bannockburn Sluicings
pois.short: A loop through eroded gold-sluicing remains that turned Central Otago hillsides into sculpted history.
pois.commentary: The Bannockburn Sluicings show gold mining as landscape surgery. Water cannons tore these gullies open in the 1800s, leaving cliffs, tailings and caves that now read like a strange outdoor museum under Central Otago’s hard light.

pois.name: Poolburn Dam
pois.short: A remote high-country reservoir among tors, tussock and big Central Otago sky.
pois.commentary: Poolburn Dam is not polished, and that is its appeal. Rough roads lead to a wind-bent reservoir set among schist tors and open tussock, where the landscape feels both cinematic and stubbornly practical, shaped by water storage, fishing, weather and space.

pois.name: Macraes Heritage and Art Park
pois.short: A goldfield village heritage walk and outdoor art stop beside one of New Zealand’s largest modern mines.
pois.commentary: Macraes is a rare place where old goldfield remnants and a modern open-cast mine sit in the same conversation. The heritage and art park gives travellers a way to walk that tension: stone ruins, big machinery nearby, and sculpture holding the middle ground.

pois.name: Doctors Point Reserve
pois.short: A Dunedin coastal reserve with tidal sea caves, surf beach and Blueskin Bay views.
pois.commentary: Doctors Point is a low-tide invitation. When the sea allows, you can walk the beach to basalt sea caves and look back across Blueskin Bay, getting a northern Dunedin coastline that feels wilder than its closeness to town suggests.

pois.name: Clutha Gold Trail - Roxburgh Gorge Gateway
pois.short: A Central Otago cycle-trail gateway following the Clutha Mata-au through orchards, gold towns and river country.
pois.commentary: The Clutha Gold Trail turns Central Otago history into a rideable line. Around Roxburgh, the river does the storytelling: orchards, old gold workings, hydro country and small towns all strung along the powerful blue-green Clutha Mata-au.

pois.name: Oamaru Blue Penguin Evening
pois.short: An evening wildlife experience watching little blue penguins return from the sea at Oamaru Harbour.
pois.commentary: Oamaru’s little blue penguins turn dusk into theatre. The birds come ashore small, determined and slightly comic after a day at sea, while managed viewing keeps people back and gives the colony a better chance to keep doing this every night.

pois.name: Ophir Suspension Bridge
pois.short: A graceful historic bridge across the Manuherikia River beside one of Central Otago’s best-preserved settlements.
pois.commentary: Ophir’s suspension bridge is a small engineering flourish in a landscape of stone, river and dry hills. Pair it with the village’s historic buildings and the place becomes a compact lesson in how Central Otago moved people, mail and goods before modern highways flattened the story.

pois.name: Gabriel’s Gully
pois.short: The Lawrence gully where Otago’s 1861 gold rush began, now read through tracks and interpretation.
pois.commentary: Gabriel’s Gully looks quiet now, which makes the gold-rush story more striking. In 1861 this little valley changed Otago’s pace almost overnight; today the paths and signs let visitors imagine the noise, hope and hard labour that once crowded the ground.

pois.name: Matanaka Farm Buildings
pois.short: A group of early farm buildings near Waikouaiti, recognised as some of New Zealand’s oldest surviving examples.
pois.commentary: Matanaka’s farm buildings are quiet, spare and powerful because of it. They hold an early farming story in timber and stone rather than drama, giving the Otago coast a physical link to the first decades of European settlement in the south.
